title: Translation of Michael Sadowsky’s paper “An elementary proof for the existence of a developable Möbius band and the attribution of the geometric problem to a variational problem”
overall_summary: This translation of Sadowsky’s 1930 note gives an elementary geometric construction of a developable Möbius band from a rectangular inextensible strip. The construction threads the strip around three circular cylindrical rods, in the worked symmetric case with diameters d, d, and 2d, producing a surface made from three planar pieces and three semicylindrical/helical pieces. Sadowsky computes the centerline geometry in an equilateral projected layout and obtains the key width estimate b ≤ l/(2√3) − 3πd/4, where l is the total midline length and 2b is the strip width; as d tends to 0, the admissible total width approaches l/√3, so the aspect ratio l/(2b) approaches √3 from above. Thus the paper is mainly useful for the upper-bound/existence direction of the original problem: it supplies a concrete developable Möbius-band family whose limiting ratio is the triangular threshold √3. The paper itself does not formulate the clean-triangulation or piecewise-affine squeeze-map setting.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Elementary existence construction of a developable Möbius band
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let a rectangular strip of flexible but completely inextensible paper be arranged with three circular cylindrical rods, with one rod having diameter equal to the sum of the diameters of the other two. Sadowsky asserts that the strip may be threaded between the rods to form a Möbius band. The resulting surface consists of three planar sections and three semicylindrical sections, and is therefore developable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The basic local module is a circular cylinder together with two parallel tangent planes: a strip can lie in one tangent plane, wrap halfway around the rod along a semicylindrical surface, and then lie in the other tangent plane. Applying this module to three rods with compatible diameters allows the strip to pass successively through three half-cylinder transitions and three planar portions, closing with Möbius connectivity. Since planes and circular cylinders are developable surfaces, each piece of the assembled surface is developable; the paper does not dwell on the detailed angle and length choices for this qualitative existence claim, stating that only existence is needed at this point.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equilateral d,d,2d centerline formulas
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the special case where the three rod diameters are d, d, and 2d, assume the projections of the midlines of the three rectangular planar portions lie on the edges of an equilateral triangle PQR of side length L, and assume the axes of all three rods are parallel to the drawing plane. Let A, B, C, D, E, F be points on the midline of the Möbius band, with projections A′, B′, C′, D′, E′, F′ onto the drawing plane. Let AB, BC, etc. denote distances along the midline, and let A′B′, B′C′, etc. denote rectilinear distances between the projected points. The sections AF, BC, and DE are rectilinear, while AB, CD, and EF are helical curves on the cylinders. If these helices meet the cylinder generators at angle 60°, then Sadowsky obtains AB = 2πd/√3, A′B′ = πd/√3, CD = EF = πd/√3, C′D′ = E′F′ = πd/(2√3), AF = BC = L − √3πd/2, DE = L − πd/√3, and the total midline length l satisfies l = 3L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the helix AB on the cylinder of diameter 2d, the half-circumference traversed around the cylinder is πd. The 60° incidence with the generators gives the trigonometric relations A′B′ = AB cos 60° and AB cos 30° = πd, whence AB = 2πd/√3 and A′B′ = πd/√3. For each helix on a cylinder of diameter d, the half-circumference is πd/2; the same 60° geometry gives CD = EF = πd/√3 and projected lengths C′D′ = E′F′ = πd/(2√3). The planar projected portions fill the remainders of the equilateral triangle sides: the sides containing one large-cylinder projected helix and one small-cylinder projected helix leave length L − πd/√3 − πd/(2√3) = L − √3πd/2, giving AF = BC; the remaining side loses two small-cylinder projected lengths, giving DE = L − πd/√3. Summing AB + BC + CD + DE + EF + FA cancels the d-dependent terms and gives l = 3L.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Width estimate and limiting √3 aspect ratio
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the same d,d,2d construction, after choosing total midline length l = 3L and rod parameter d, the points A, B, C, D, E, F occur in the required order provided 3√3πd ≤ 2l. If the strip has total width 2b, the cylinder generators are marked on the strip at angle 60°, and the portions wound around different cylinders are required not to overlap, then the half-width b must satisfy b ≤ (BC/2) tan 60° = l/(2√3) − 3πd/4. Thus the admissibility constraint is d ≤ 2l/(3√3π). Consequently, for fixed l, the maximal total width 2b in this construction tends to l/√3 as d ↓ 0, so the aspect ratio l/(2b) can be made arbitrarily close to √3 from above. In the infinitely narrow limiting case treated by Sadowsky, one sets BC = AF = 0; then AB = 4l/9, CD = EF = 2l/9, and DE = l/9.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: From the centerline formulas, the shortest unbent portions are BC = AF = L − √3πd/2 = l/3 − √3πd/2. Requiring the named points to occur in order forces these lengths to be nonnegative, which is equivalent to 3√3πd ≤ 2l. For the width estimate, the generators on the strip make angle 60° with the midline; crossing half the width b therefore consumes longitudinal distance b/tan 60° along a planar portion. Since two adjacent cylindrical portions can intrude into the same shortest planar gap, non-overlap requires 2b/tan 60° ≤ BC, equivalently b ≤ (BC/2)tan 60°. Substituting BC = l/3 − √3πd/2 and tan 60° = √3 gives b ≤ l/(2√3) − 3πd/4. Nonnegative admissible width gives the same bound d ≤ 2l/(3√3π). Letting d tend to 0 yields maximal total width 2b tending to l/√3. In the b = 0 limiting calculation Sadowsky sets BC = AF = 0, which saturates the d-constraint; substituting d = 2l/(3√3π) into the helical-length formulas gives AB = 4l/9, CD = EF = 2l/9, and DE = l/9.

proof_techniques.name: Plane–half-cylinder–plane developable modules
proof_techniques.description: Sadowsky’s fundamental construction step is to move an inextensible strip from one plane to a parallel tangent plane by wrapping it halfway around a circular cylinder. This produces a piecewise developable surface: the incoming and outgoing parts are planar, and the transition is a semicylindrical patch. Three such transitions, arranged around rods with compatible diameters, close the strip with Möbius one-sided connectivity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original problem’s upper-bound direction, this suggests building a quotient strip from three repeated transition zones separated by planar zones. A solver could try to replace the smooth cylindrical transition zones by triangulated or ruled approximations while preserving the same three-transition Möbius topology.

proof_techniques.name: Equilateral projected skeleton with 60° helical turns
proof_techniques.description: Rather than parameterizing the entire surface, Sadowsky tracks the midline and its projection onto a drawing plane. In the d,d,2d case, the projected straight portions lie along the edges of an equilateral triangle, while the twisted portions are helices meeting cylinder generators at 60°. Elementary cosine relations then determine the lengths of the helical pieces and their projected lengths, and subtracting these projected lengths from the equilateral side length gives the planar segment lengths.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem’s threshold √3 is naturally tied to equilateral and 60° geometry. This technique points toward setting up a triangular fundamental skeleton for a Gβ-quotient strip, with length inequalities reduced to elementary equilateral-triangle computations.

proof_techniques.name: Maximal width controlled by the shortest planar gap
proof_techniques.description: Sadowsky obtains the strip-width constraint by marking the cylinder generators on the band at angle 60° and requiring different wound portions not to overlap. The bottleneck is the shortest unbent portion, BC = AF. Since a generator crossing half-width b at angle 60° uses longitudinal distance b/tan 60°, two neighboring wound regions fit into the planar gap only when 2b/tan 60° ≤ BC, giving b ≤ (BC/2)tan 60°.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the paper’s most direct source of the √3 threshold. With the original strip width normalized, the estimate suggests that any construction for β > √3 should leave a positive gap analogous to BC after imposing 60° generator geometry.

proof_techniques.name: Approach the critical ratio by a degenerating rod parameter
proof_techniques.description: The diameter parameter d controls how much midline length is spent in the cylindrical/helical transition regions. The width bound b ≤ l/(2√3) − 3πd/4 tends to b ≤ l/(2√3) as d tends to 0, so the construction gives bands whose aspect ratio approaches √3 from above but does not require realizing the limiting collapsed object as a positive-width smooth band. At the limiting b = 0 computation, the midline segment lengths have fixed proportions 4/9, 2/9, 1/9, 2/9, with two planar gaps collapsed.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For proving an infimum, this supplies a standard strategy: construct examples with a small positive degeneration parameter for every β > √3, and reserve the limiting β = √3 object as a guide for the lower-bound or nonattainment analysis.

other_useful_info: Sadowsky also formulates the ideal smooth shape of an unconstrained developable Möbius band as a variational problem: minimize the bending energy ∫∫ H^2 dF over the whole surface, where H is mean curvature, subject to the strip being rectangular, developable, and having the proper one-sided connectivity. For an infinitesimally narrow band with midline arclength parameter s, curvature K, and torsion W, he states that this reduces to minimizing ∫_0^l ((K^2 + W^2)^2/K^2) ds; he also records ∫_0^l H^2 ds = 15π^2/l as an upper bound associated with the narrow limiting construction. The paper notes that the constructed plane-and-cylinder band has discontinuous curvature and would deform under removal of supporting forces; an addendum points to Sadowsky’s report at the 3rd International Congress for Technical Mechanics, Stockholm 1930, for further determination of the configuration.
why_relevant: Sadowsky’s construction is the classical existence mechanism for developable paper Möbius bands. It is useful for the upper-bound direction, i.e. building examples for aspect ratios above the triangular threshold.

title: Translation and interpretation of Michael Sadowsky’s paper “Theory of elastically bendable inextensible bands with applications to the MÖBIUS band”
overall_summary: This paper is a translation and interpretation of Sadowsky’s 1930 work on elastically bendable inextensible bands, especially narrow developable Möbius bands. Its main mathematical mechanism is to encode an inextensible band by the Frenet triad of its midline, derive the admissible infinitesimal rotation of that material triad, and then use virtual work plus Frenet–Serret equations to obtain the Sadowsky energy density and the equilibrium differential equations for curvature and torsion. The geometrically most relevant part relates the angle of the rectilinear generators of the developable band to the ratio torsion/curvature, and discusses a symmetry-induced singular point at which limiting generators meet a flat right-triangular patch at 45 degrees. For the original discrete problem, the paper is useful mainly as a smooth analogue: clean triangles and squeeze maps can be viewed as a polyhedral/developable model of a Möbius-type strip, and Sadowsky’s ruling-angle, compatibility, and singular-triangle ideas may suggest the right discrete geometric invariants or lower-bound certificates.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 1: Kinematic characterization of a band by a material Frenet triad
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let r(s) be the midline of a body, parametrized by arclength s, and let t, n, and b denote its tangent, normal, and binormal Frenet vectors. Sadowsky defines a band as an inextensible body whose midline is material and whose full accompanying triad t, n, b is material. Geometrically, the band is taken to be part of the rectifying surface of its midline: the rectifying plane at r(s) is the plane spanned by t and b, and the rectifying surface is the envelope of these planes. The midline is also required to become a straight line when the band is developed to the plane, equivalently the midline is a geodesic on the band surface. Together with the inextensibility condition δ(ds)=0, the materiality of the triad is presented as the defining kinematic property of a band.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper contrasts a band with an isotropic rope or wire. For a rope or wire only the tangent t is material, because bending is isotropic and the normal/binormal directions have no constitutive meaning. For a band, the surface tangent plane is material, the tangent t to the material midline is material, and the binormal b lies in that material tangent plane because the band is part of the rectifying surface and the midline is a geodesic. Since b is a direction in a material plane perpendicular to the material tangent direction, b is material; then n=b×t is also material. Thus the whole Frenet triad is fixed to the matter of the band. Sadowsky then takes this triad materiality plus δ(ds)=0 as the kinematic definition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2, Equation (10): Compatible virtual rotation of an inextensible band
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let r(s) be the inextensible midline of a band, with Frenet triad t,n,b, curvature K(s), and torsion W(s), satisfying the Frenet–Serret formulas t′=K n, n′=−K t+W b, and b′=−W n, where prime denotes d/ds. Under an admissible virtual deformation of the band, suppose the curvature and torsion vary to K+δK and W+δW, and suppose the material triad at arclength s undergoes an infinitesimal rotation vector δϑ(s). Then the change dδϑ in this virtual rotation between s and s+ds is constrained by dδϑ=(t δW+b δK) ds. Equivalently, it is the general solution of the three vector equations n δK ds=dδϑ×t, −t δK ds+b δW ds=dδϑ×n, and −n δW ds=dδϑ×b.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose two neighboring midline points P=r(s) and P′=r(s+ds). Because the band is inextensible, ds is unchanged by an admissible virtual deformation; let P and P′ move to Q and Q′. There are two ways to compute the triad at Q′. First move from P to P′ along the original curve, using the Frenet–Serret formulas to write the triad at P′ as t+K n ds, n+(−K t+W b)ds, b−W n ds, and then rotate this triad by δϑ+dδϑ. Second rotate the triad at P by δϑ to get the triad at Q, then move from Q to Q′ along the deformed curve, whose curvature and torsion are K+δK and W+δW. Equating the two resulting triads and neglecting all terms of third order in infinitesimals yields the three equations displayed in the statement. To solve them, write dδϑ=a t+c n+d b. Since b×t=n, n×t=−b, and t×n=b, the first equation forces c=0 and d=δK ds; the second then forces a=δW ds; the third is automatically satisfied. Hence dδϑ=(t δW+b δK)ds.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3, Equations (13)–(15): Force and moment equilibrium for a narrow band
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For an infinitesimally narrow band with no external forces, let F be the internal force and M the internal moment at a midline point r(s). Write F=T_f t+N_f n+B_f b and M=T_m t+N_m n+B_m b in the Frenet basis, where K is the curvature and W is the torsion of the midline. The equilibrium equations are F′=0 and M′+t×F=0. In components these become T_f′−K N_f=0, K T_f+N_f′−W B_f=0, W N_f+B_f′=0, T_m′−K N_m=0, K T_m+N_m′−W B_m−B_f=0, and W N_m+B_m′+N_f=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Differentiate F=T_f t+N_f n+B_f b using the Frenet–Serret formulas. The t, n, and b components of F′=0 give T_f′−K N_f=0, K T_f+N_f′−W B_f=0, and W N_f+B_f′=0. Similarly, differentiate M=T_m t+N_m n+B_m b to obtain components T_m′−K N_m, K T_m+N_m′−W B_m, and W N_m+B_m′. Since t×F=t×(T_f t+N_f n+B_f b)=N_f b−B_f n, the n-component of M′+t×F=0 acquires the extra term −B_f and the b-component acquires the extra term +N_f, giving the last two displayed equations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3, Equations (16)–(22): Virtual work and Sadowsky moment laws
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a band segment s∈[s1,s2], the internal virtual work of the moment M=T_m t+N_m n+B_m b is δAi=−∫_{s1}^{s2} M·dδϑ. Using the compatible virtual rotation dδϑ=(t δW+b δK)ds, this becomes δAi=−∫_{s1}^{s2}(T_m δW+B_m δK)ds. For a Möbius band with Sadowsky elastic potential density U=A(K^2+W^2)^2/K^2, where A>0 and K is nonzero on the interval considered, the elastic energy is E=∫U ds and its variation is δE=∫[(∂U/∂W)δW+(∂U/∂K)δK]ds. Since δAi=−δE and δK,δW are independent variations, the moment components are T_m=4A W(K^2+W^2)/K^2 and B_m=2A(K^4−W^4)/K^3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper invokes the principle of virtual displacements for a one-dimensional continuum with material midline. Shear force, and also tensile force for an inextensible band, do no virtual work; the remaining internal virtual work is the moment contribution −∫M·dδϑ. Substitution of the kinematic identity dδϑ=(t δW+b δK)ds gives −∫(T_m δW+B_m δK)ds, because only the t- and b-components of M pair with dδϑ. For the Sadowsky potential U=A(K^2+W^2)^2/K^2, direct differentiation gives ∂U/∂W=4A W(K^2+W^2)/K^2 and ∂U/∂K=2A(K^4−W^4)/K^3. Comparing coefficients in δAi=−δE yields the two moment laws.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3, Equations (23)–(27): Reduction of the Möbius-band equilibrium equations to curvature and torsion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K(s) and W(s) be the curvature and torsion of the midline of a Sadowsky Möbius band, let A>0 be the elastic constant, and define S=(K^2+W^2)^2/K^2 and P=W(K^2+W^2)/K^2. With the moment laws T_m=4A P and B_m=2A(K^4−W^4)/K^3, the force and moment coefficients can be expressed as T_f=A(C−S), N_f=−(A/K)S′, B_f=2A W(K^2+W^2)^2/K^3+4A d/ds[(1/K)P′], and N_m=(4A/K)P′, where C is an arbitrary integration constant. The remaining equilibrium conditions are K T_f+N_f′−W B_f=0 and W N_f+B_f′=0; substituting the displayed formulae into these two equations gives two differential equations for K and W.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start from the six component equilibrium equations and the two moment laws. Since T_m=4A P, the equation T_m′−K N_m=0 gives N_m=4A P′/K. The b-component of moment balance, W N_m+B_m′+N_f=0, determines N_f; after differentiating the displayed B_m and simplifying, this is N_f=−(A/K)S′. The n-component of moment balance, K T_m+N_m′−W B_m−B_f=0, determines B_f and gives B_f=2A W(K^2+W^2)^2/K^3+4A d/ds[(1/K)P′]. The first force-balance equation T_f′−K N_f=0 then becomes T_f′=−A S′, so T_f=A(C−S) for an integration constant C. The two force-balance equations not yet used are precisely K T_f+N_f′−W B_f=0 and W N_f+B_f′=0, and these are the promised two differential equations for K and W.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4 and Appendix A, Equation (28): Generator-angle formula
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: At a point P on the midline of the developable band, let φ be the angle between the rectilinear generator of the band through P and the binormal b at P. If K is the curvature and W is the torsion of the midline at P, then Sadowsky states tan φ=W/K. The appendix explains this as a geometric consequence of the fact that the midline tangent cuts the rectilinear generators at angle 90 degrees ±φ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper gives only a brief geometric justification, but it is consistent with the rectifying-surface description from Section 1. The generator direction lies in the plane spanned by t and b, so with the stated convention it may be written, up to orientation, as g=cos φ b+sin φ t. For the ruled surface r(s)+u g(s) to be developable, the vectors r′=t, g, and g′ must be linearly dependent. Differentiating g with the Frenet–Serret formulas shows that the n-component of g′ is −W cos φ+K sin φ, while the terms involving φ′ lie in the span of t and b. Linear dependence therefore forces −W cos φ+K sin φ=0, which is tan φ=W/K.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4: Sadowsky’s symmetry-point and right-triangle observation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Sadowsky asserts that the midline of a Möbius band possesses a singular point X found using a 180-degree rotational symmetry axis of the band. This symmetry axis intersects the band at two points and coincides with the binormal b at one of them, denoted X. At X, symmetry gives B_m=0, where B_m is the b-component of the moment, equal in the Sadowsky law to 2A(K^4−W^4)/K^3. Sadowsky further reports model-based observations that as P approaches X, the generator angle φ has nonzero limit and the twisting moment T_m has nonzero limit. From these facts and the formulas for T_m and B_m he concludes that, in the limiting sense considered there, K and W do not tend to zero and φ tends to 45 degrees. He states that this implies the presence of a planar right triangle: the curved analytic portion of the band connects continuously to the two legs of this right triangle, with continuous tangent plane but discontinuous curvature.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The symmetry condition gives B_m=0 at the distinguished point. Using B_m=2A(K^4−W^4)/K^3, nonzero limiting curvature excludes the zero solution and forces K^4=W^4, hence W/K=±1. By the generator-angle formula tan φ=W/K, this yields φ=±45 degrees. The additional observation that T_m=4A W(K^2+W^2)/K^2 has nonzero limiting value is used by Sadowsky to rule out a trivial vanishing configuration in the limiting argument. The conclusion about a planar right triangle is stated tersely in the original Section 4: the 45-degree limiting generators form the right-triangular planar part, while the nonzero limiting curvature/torsion of the adjacent analytic portion produce a curvature discontinuity at the connection.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Appendix A: Interpretation of the singular point X as a flat point with limiting 45-degree generators
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The appendix interprets and partly revises Sadowsky’s Section 4 as follows. At the actual singular point X, the curvature and torsion satisfy K=0 and W=0. The reasoning is: B_m=0 at X gives K^4−W^4=0; if K were nonzero at X, then W/K=±1 and the generator-angle formula would give φ=±45 degrees, implying two perpendicular rectilinear generators through X and hence flatness at X, contradicting K≠0. For nearby nonflat points P in the relevant punctured neighborhood of X, K is nonzero; the appendix argues that W is also nonzero in the limiting configuration being discussed, so the nonzero torsion/curvature region approaches the flat singular point with generators limiting to ±45 degrees. This limiting geometry is said to correspond to the planar right-triangle feature observed in simulations.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The appendix begins with the same symmetry condition B_m=0 and substitutes the Sadowsky expression B_m=2A(K^4−W^4)/K^3 to get K^4−W^4=0. If K≠0, then W/K=±1, so tan φ=±1 and φ=±π/4. The appendix states that this would place two perpendicular rectilinear generators of the bent surface at X, which would force the surface to be flat there. That contradicts the assumption K≠0 at X, so K=0; then K^4−W^4=0 also gives W=0. For a nearby point P, if K were zero while the generator direction and midline tangent were distinct, then the surface would have zero curvature along both the generator and the tangent direction and would be flat at P, contrary to being in the bent part; hence K≠0 in the nonflat punctured neighborhood. The appendix then explains Sadowsky’s intended limiting picture: K and W vanish at X itself but are nonzero on nearby points approaching X, and the associated rectilinear generators approach the flat point at ±45 degrees, forming the planar right-triangle patch.

proof_techniques.name: Encode an inextensible surface by a material moving frame
proof_techniques.description: Sadowsky reduces the kinematics of a narrow inextensible band to the Frenet frame of its midline. The central point is not merely that the midline is inextensible, but that the whole triad t,n,b is material: the surface tangent plane and the binormal direction are tied to the band’s matter. This converts a surface deformation problem into constraints on a curve, its curvature K, its torsion W, and the infinitesimal rotation δϑ of a moving orthonormal frame.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the clean-triangulated strip, one can try to replace the smooth material triad by discrete data attached to triangles or boundary vertices, such as boundary-edge directions, cross-strip diagonals, and triangle normals. The squeeze-map inequalities may then be studied as compatibility conditions for a discrete material frame on the quotient by the glide reflection G_beta.

proof_techniques.name: Two-path comparison around an infinitesimal strip element
proof_techniques.description: The derivation of dδϑ=(t δW+b δK)ds compares two ways of reaching the same neighboring deformed frame: move along the original midline and then deform, or deform first and then move along the deformed midline. Equating the two results after using the Frenet–Serret equations gives a local compatibility identity. This is essentially a commutation-of-variation-with-arclength calculation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A discrete analogue would compare images obtained by crossing adjacent triangles in different orders or by applying G_beta before versus after moving through a fundamental triangulation. Such local compatibility identities are plausible tools for proving lower bounds on beta from triangle-by-triangle squeeze constraints.

proof_techniques.name: Convert developability into a ruling-angle formula
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the rectifying-developable structure to relate the generator angle φ to the curve invariants K and W via tan φ=W/K. In geometric terms, a generator lies in the span of the tangent and binormal; developability forces the derivative of the generator to stay in the tangent plane of the ruled surface, which determines its angle from the torsion/curvature ratio.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Clean triangles have straight edges with vertices on the boundary of the strip, so their affine images may behave like a polyhedral version of rulings. A useful strategy is to search for a discrete ruling-angle invariant or monotonicity relation that turns the global Möbius identification into a metric inequality involving beta.

proof_techniques.name: Virtual-work duality and equilibrium coefficients
proof_techniques.description: Sadowsky’s smooth proof introduces internal force and moment components, derives equilibrium equations, and then uses virtual work to identify the moment components as partial derivatives of an energy density with respect to torsion and curvature. The subsequent reduction expresses all force and moment coefficients in terms of K, W, and their derivatives, leaving two scalar differential equations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original problem has no energy functional, the same duality idea may inspire a discrete stress certificate: assign forces or weights to edges/triangles so that equilibrium plus squeeze inequalities imply a global lower bound on beta. This could be especially useful for proving non-realizability below sqrt(3).

proof_techniques.name: Exploit Möbius symmetry to force a singular flat patch
proof_techniques.description: The paper’s geometric discussion uses a 180-degree rotational symmetry axis of the Möbius band to identify a special point X where a moment component vanishes. Combining this with the Sadowsky constitutive law and the ruling-angle identity yields limiting 45-degree generators and a planar right-triangular patch attached to the curved part with continuous tangent plane but discontinuous curvature.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The quotient of Sigma by the glide reflection G_beta is a Möbius-type strip, so a discrete proof may benefit from identifying an unavoidable special orbit, triangle, or diagonal playing the role of X. The planar right-triangle feature suggests looking for a forced triangular configuration whose elementary geometry produces the critical constant sqrt(3).

proof_techniques.name: Use singular limits rather than assuming global smoothness
proof_techniques.description: Sadowsky’s Section 4 and the appendix emphasize that an exactly developable Möbius band may have a singular point where curvature and torsion behavior is not smoothly continued. The limiting generators and tangent planes still carry decisive geometric information even when curvature is discontinuous or vanishes at the point itself.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The clean triangulation model is inherently nonsmooth, so singularities should not be treated as defects to avoid. A proof can deliberately use vertices, flat patches, or discontinuities between affine triangles as carriers of the essential obstruction.

other_useful_info: The paper cites Sadowsky [1], “Ein elementarer Beweis für die Existenz eines abwickelbaren Möbiusschen Bandes und die Zurückführung des geometrischen Problems auf ein Variationsproblem”, for an elementary proof of existence of an exactly developable Möbius band and reduction to a variational problem; that cited work may be closer to the existence side of the original problem. The virtual-work background is attributed to Hamel [2–5]. Appendix A relies partly on numerical simulations by Kleiman, Hinz, and Fried [6], which show the singular point X with K=0 and W=0 and visually exhibit planar right-triangular regions, especially at lower aspect ratios. The paper itself is smooth and differential-geometric; it contains no direct theorem about clean triangulations, squeeze maps, or the parameter beta.
why_relevant: This contains Sadowsky’s foundational differential-geometric model of inextensible ribbons and Möbius bands. The discussion of rectifying developables, rulings, and bending energy is relevant to the smooth theory underlying the discrete squeeze-map model.

title: The Optimal Paper Moebius Band
overall_summary: The paper proves the Halpern-Weaver conjecture: every smooth embedded paper Moebius band of aspect ratio λ has λ > √3, and any sequence with λ tending to √3 converges, up to isometry, to the triangular Moebius band. The proof has two main parts: a topological Borsuk-Ulam argument produces an embedded T-pattern, i.e. two disjoint bends whose supporting lines are perpendicular and intersect; then a cut-open trapezoid geometry argument converts the existence of this T-pattern into the sharp inequality λ > √3. The key quantitative step is a one-variable optimization using two inequalities δ(t) < 2λ and ν(t) < 2λ, whose balanced minimum occurs at t = 1/√3 and gives 2λ > 2√3. For the original problem, the most portable ingredients are the abstract T-pattern detection mechanism for cyclic families of oriented lines, the trapezoid/triangle length estimates after cutting along one cross-segment, and the triangular limiting model that realizes the sharp constant.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1 (Main)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let λ > 0, and let M_λ be the flat Moebius band obtained from the rectangle [0, λ] × [0, 1] by identifying (0, y) with (λ, 1 − y). A smooth paper Moebius band of aspect ratio λ is an infinitely differentiable arc-length-preserving map I: M_λ → R^3, and it is embedded when I is injective. If I: M_λ → R^3 is a smooth embedded paper Moebius band, then λ > √3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is the direct combination of two lemmas. First, Proposition 2.2 gives a continuous foliation of Ω = I(M_λ) by bends, where a bend is a line segment in Ω cutting across Ω with endpoints on ∂Ω. Lemma 1.3 then applies a Borsuk-Ulam argument to this bend foliation and produces an embedded T-pattern: two distinct, disjoint bends whose supporting lines are perpendicular and intersect. Lemma 1.4 shows that any smooth embedded paper Moebius band possessing such an embedded T-pattern must have λ > √3. Thus every smooth embedded paper Moebius band satisfies λ > √3.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2 (Triangular Limit)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let {I_n: M_{λ_n} → Ω_n} be a sequence of smooth embedded paper Moebius bands, where M_{λ_n} = ([0, λ_n] × [0, 1]) / ((0, y) ~ (λ_n, 1 − y)), and assume λ_n → √3. Then, after applying suitable Euclidean isometries, I_n converges uniformly to the piecewise-linear map giving the triangular Moebius band.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each n, run the construction used in Lemma 1.4. Choose an embedded T-pattern, cut M_{λ_n} open along one pre-bend T_n, and obtain a bilaterally symmetric trapezoid τ_n with a horizontal-displacement parameter t_n. Lemma 1.4 gives 2λ_n > max(δ(t_n), ν(t_n)), where δ(t) = √(1 + t^2) + √(5 + t^2) and ν(t) = 2√(5 + t^2) − 2t. Since max(δ(t), ν(t)) has its unique minimum 2√3 at t = 1/√3, and 2λ_n → 2√3, one gets t_n → 1/√3. Consequently the length of the image bend T'_n tends to 2/√3. The triangle ∇_n used in Lemma 1.4 has base tending to 2/√3, height always greater than 1, and perimeter at most 2λ_n; Lemma 2.3 forces ∇_n, after normalization, to converge to the isosceles triangle of base 2/√3 and height 1, which is equilateral. The slack in the length inequalities then vanishes: the relevant boundary arcs H'_{n,1}, H'_{n,2}, D'_{n,1}, D'_{n,2} have lengths tending respectively to 1/√3, 1/√3, 2/√3, 2/√3. Hence the cut-open domains τ_n converge to the trapezoid underlying the triangular Moebius band, and the boundary maps converge uniformly to the corresponding piecewise-linear boundary map. Finally, divide M_{λ_n} into three triangles. On the cut segment T_n the map is linear isometry; on the relevant boundary segments it converges to linear isometry; and on the remaining edges Euclidean distances converge to intrinsic edge lengths because I_n is distance non-increasing. Therefore I_n converges on each of the three triangles to the linear isometry defining the triangular Moebius band. The paper further observes that the bend foliations themselves converge to the pinstriping of the triangular model.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.3 (T)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let I: M_λ → Ω ⊂ R^3 be a smooth embedded paper Moebius band. A T-pattern on Ω is a pair of bends lying in perpendicular intersecting supporting lines; it is embedded if the two bends are disjoint as subsets of Ω. Then Ω has an embedded T-pattern.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a continuous bend foliation β of Ω, supplied by Proposition 2.2. First show that every pre-bend in M_λ intersects the centerline of M_λ exactly once. If a pre-bend u has length less than √(1 + λ^2), it can be represented in the rectangle without crossing the vertical sides, where the assertion is clear. If u intersected the centerline more than once, then its length would be at least √(1 + λ^2) > λ. But ∂Ω is a single loop of length 2λ containing the endpoints of the image bend u' = I(u); the two boundary arcs between these endpoints each have length at least ℓ(u'), so 2λ ≥ 2ℓ(u') = 2ℓ(u) > 2λ, a contradiction. Thus bends can be parametrized by their unique intersection with the centerline, identified with R/2π. Let Υ be the cylinder of ordered pairs (x_0, x_1) of distinct bend parameters. Compactify Υ by two points ∂_+ and ∂_−, corresponding respectively to x_1 approaching x_0 from just ahead or just behind in the cyclic order; the compactification is S^2. The coordinate-swap involution Σ(x_0, x_1) = (x_1, x_0) extends to S^2 and swaps ∂_+ and ∂_−. For a pair (x_0, x_1), move positively from x_0 to x_1 along the parameter circle by a path of length less than 2π, and propagate an orientation of bends continuously along this path. Write the resulting oriented unit vectors as →u_0 and →u_1, with midpoints m_0 and m_1. Define F = (g, h): Υ → R^2 by g(x_0, x_1) = →u_0 · →u_1 and h(x_0, x_1) = (m_0 − m_1) · (→u_0 × →u_1). Simultaneous reversal of both orientations does not change F. Since Ω is a Moebius band, reversing the order of the pair changes the propagated orientation by a sign, and one checks F ∘ Σ = −F. Also F extends continuously to S^2 with F(∂_±) = (±1, 0). By the Borsuk-Ulam theorem, the odd map F has a zero in S^2; since F(∂_±) ≠ 0, the zero lies in Υ. At such a zero, g = 0 gives perpendicular directions. The equation h = 0 says the vector between midpoints has zero scalar triple product with the two direction vectors, so the two supporting lines are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. Since the corresponding bends are distinct leaves of a foliation, they are disjoint. Hence they form an embedded T-pattern.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.4 (G)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let I: M_λ → Ω ⊂ R^3 be a smooth embedded paper Moebius band of aspect ratio λ. If Ω has an embedded T-pattern, then λ > √3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let T' and B' be the two bends in an embedded T-pattern, and let T = I^{-1}(T') and B = I^{-1}(B') be the corresponding pre-bends. After a rigid motion of R^3, arrange that T' lies in the X-axis and B' lies in the negative ray of the Y-axis. Cut M_λ open along T. The result is a bilaterally symmetric trapezoid τ, normalized so that its parallel sides are horizontal. In the notation of Figure 2 of the paper, t is the horizontal displacement of T in τ, b is the horizontal displacement of B, and H = H_1 ∪ H_2 and D = D_1 ∪ D_2 are the two boundary arcs of τ connecting the endpoints of the two copies of T. The image triangle ∇ with base T' has base length ℓ(T') = ℓ(T) = √(1 + t^2), and its height is greater than ℓ(B') = ℓ(B) = √(1 + b^2) ≥ 1. Lemma 2.3 applied to ∇ gives a strict lower bound √(5 + t^2) < ℓ(∨), where ∨ denotes the two non-horizontal sides of ∇. Since those sides are no longer than the corresponding image boundary arc D', one has √(5 + t^2) < ℓ(D') = ℓ(D). The trapezoid geometry and arc-length preservation also give ℓ(H) + ℓ(D) = 2λ, ℓ(D) − 2t = ℓ(H), and √(1 + t^2) = ℓ(T') ≤ ℓ(H') = ℓ(H). Therefore δ(t) := √(1 + t^2) + √(5 + t^2) < 2λ and ν(t) := 2√(5 + t^2) − 2t < 2λ. Let t_0 = 1/√3. Then δ(t_0) = ν(t_0) = 2√3, δ is increasing on (0, ∞), and ν is decreasing on R. Thus if t > t_0 then δ(t) > 2√3, while if t < t_0 then ν(t) > 2√3; at t = t_0 the inequalities are strict. In all cases 2λ > 2√3, so λ > √3.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let I: M_λ → Ω be a smooth paper Moebius band, and let U ⊂ Ω be the set of points at which Ω has nonzero mean curvature. Then each point p ∈ U lies in a unique bend γ ⊂ U.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because Ω is locally isometric to the flat band, its Gaussian curvature is zero. Let η be the Gauss map, locally defined on U. At points of U, the mean curvature is nonzero, so the differential dη has one-dimensional image and one-dimensional kernel in each tangent plane. Let γ be the integral curve through p tangent to ker(dη). Along γ, η is constant. The orthonormal frame consisting of ker(dη), im(dη), and η is then constant along γ: η is constant by construction, im(dη) is constant because η is constant along a foliation by these integral curves, and therefore ker(dη) is constant as well. Thus γ is a straight line segment. Standard continuation gives that γ extends in both directions until it reaches ∂Ω, and uniqueness follows from uniqueness of the integral curve through p.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let I: M_λ → Ω be a smooth embedded paper Moebius band. Then Ω has a continuous foliation by bends, i.e. Ω is covered by pairwise disjoint line segments cutting across Ω with endpoints in ∂Ω, varying continuously from leaf to leaf.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: On U, the set of nonzero mean curvature points, Lemma 2.1 gives a foliation by bends. No two such bends can intersect, even at the boundary: if two distinct straight bend segments met, the intersection point would have two distinct straight directions on the surface, forcing zero mean curvature there, contrary to being in U. This disjointness implies continuity of the foliation on U. Now consider the closure τ of a connected component of Ω − U. If τ has empty interior, it is a line segment obtained as a limit of bends, hence is itself taken as a bend. If τ has nonempty interior, then the Gauss map is constant on τ, so τ lies in a plane. Moreover τ is a trapezoid whose two opposite sides are bends and whose other two opposite sides lie in ∂Ω. Foliate such a planar trapezoid by line segments interpolating continuously between the two bend sides; the interpolation uses segments lying on parallel or coincident lines according as the two boundary bends do. Performing this filling for all components of Ω − U extends the foliation to all of Ω.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Centerline intersection fact in the proof of Lemma 1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a smooth embedded paper Moebius band I: M_λ → Ω with a bend foliation, every pre-bend u = I^{-1}(u') in M_λ intersects the centerline of M_λ exactly once, where the centerline is the image of [0, λ] × {1/2} under the Moebius identification.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If ℓ(u) < √(1 + λ^2), then u can be moved by an isometry of the flat universal rectangle so that it avoids the vertical sides of [0, λ] × [0, 1]; in that representative, a straight segment cutting from one boundary component of the rectangle to the other plainly crosses the horizontal midline exactly once. If u met the centerline more than once, then its length would have to be at least √(1 + λ^2), hence greater than λ. But the boundary ∂Ω is a single closed curve of length 2λ containing the two endpoints of the straight image segment u'. The two boundary arcs between these endpoints each have length at least the Euclidean distance between the endpoints, which equals ℓ(u') = ℓ(u). Hence 2λ = ℓ(∂Ω) ≥ 2ℓ(u) > 2λ, impossible. This proves uniqueness of the centerline intersection and enables the parametrization of bends by S^1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ▽ be a Euclidean triangle with horizontal base of length √(1 + t^2) and height h ≥ 1. Let ∨ be the union of the two non-horizontal sides of ▽. Then ℓ(∨) ≥ √(5 + t^2). Equality holds if and only if ▽ is isosceles and h = 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let p_1 and p_2 be the endpoints of the horizontal base, and q the third vertex. Reflect p_2 across the horizontal line through q to a point p'_2. Then ℓ(∨) = ||p_1 − q|| + ||q − p'_2||. By the triangle inequality, this is at least ||p_1 − p'_2||, with equality exactly when p_1, q, p'_2 are collinear, which is equivalent to ▽ being isosceles. The distance ||p_1 − p'_2|| has horizontal component equal to the base length √(1 + t^2) and vertical component 2h, so ||p_1 − p'_2|| = √(1 + t^2 + 4h^2) ≥ √(5 + t^2). Equality in the final inequality requires h = 1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equations (3)–(5) in Lemma 1.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the cut-open trapezoid setup associated to an embedded T-pattern on a smooth embedded paper Moebius band I: M_λ → Ω, let t be the horizontal displacement parameter of the pre-bend T, and let H = H_1 ∪ H_2 and D = D_1 ∪ D_2 be the two boundary arcs of the cut-open trapezoid between the endpoints of the two copies of T. Then the paper derives ℓ(H) + ℓ(D) = 2λ, ℓ(D) − 2t = ℓ(H), √(1 + t^2) = ℓ(T') ≤ ℓ(H') = ℓ(H), and √(5 + t^2) < ℓ(D') = ℓ(D). Consequently, with δ(t) = √(1 + t^2) + √(5 + t^2) and ν(t) = 2√(5 + t^2) − 2t, one has 2λ > max(δ(t), ν(t)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first identity is simply that H and D together are the full boundary of M_λ, whose length is 2λ. The second identity is a planar trapezoid relation in the cut-open domain: the two boundary arcs differ by the two horizontal offsets, giving ℓ(D) − 2t = ℓ(H). The inequality √(1 + t^2) = ℓ(T') ≤ ℓ(H') = ℓ(H) uses arc-length preservation and the fact that H' is a curve in R^3 connecting the endpoints of the straight segment T'. The final strict inequality comes from the triangle ∇ in the T-pattern geometry: its base is T' of length √(1 + t^2), its height is greater than 1, so Lemma 2.3 gives √(5 + t^2) < ℓ(∨), and the broken side ∨ is no longer than the boundary image arc D'. Adding the third and fourth inequalities gives δ(t) < ℓ(H) + ℓ(D) = 2λ. Doubling the fourth inequality and using ℓ(H) = ℓ(D) − 2t gives ν(t) < 2ℓ(D) − 2t = ℓ(D) + ℓ(H) = 2λ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Triangular-limit estimates, Equations (6)–(7)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the notation of the proof of Theorem 1.2, for a sequence of smooth embedded paper Moebius bands I_n: M_{λ_n} → Ω_n with λ_n → √3, the associated T-pattern construction gives t_n → 1/√3, ℓ(T'_n) → 2/√3, limsup ℓ(∇_n) ≤ 2√3, and the boundary arc limits ℓ(H'_{n,1}) → 1/√3, ℓ(H'_{n,2}) → 1/√3, ℓ(D'_{n,1}) → 2/√3, ℓ(D'_{n,2}) → 2/√3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: From Lemma 1.4, 2λ_n > max(δ(t_n), ν(t_n)), while max(δ(t), ν(t)) is always at least 2√3 and equals 2√3 only at t = 1/√3. Since 2λ_n → 2√3, this squeezes max(δ(t_n), ν(t_n)) to 2√3 and forces t_n → 1/√3. Then ℓ(T'_n) = √(1 + t_n^2) → 2/√3. The triangle ∇_n used in the proof has perimeter bounded above by the full boundary length 2λ_n, so limsup ℓ(∇_n) ≤ 2√3. Its base tends to 2/√3 and its height is always greater than 1, so Lemma 2.3 forces convergence to the equality case: an isosceles triangle of height 1, hence an equilateral triangle. Since the boundary length outside ∇_n has vanishing slack and the apex converges to the midpoint position dictated by the equality case, the individual boundary arcs converge to the stated lengths.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.1 explicit homeomorphism for the pair compactification
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let the bends of a chosen bend foliation be parametrized by X_θ for θ ∈ R/2π. Let Υ be the space of ordered pairs of distinct bends, compactified by two points ∂_+ and ∂_− corresponding to the two cyclic ways for the second bend to approach the first. Identify ∂_+ and ∂_− with the north and south poles of S^2, and parametrize S^2 − {∂_+, ∂_−} by longitude θ ∈ R/2π and latitude angle φ ∈ (0, π). Then the map (θ, φ) ↦ (X_{θ−φ}, X_{θ+φ}) is a homeomorphism from S^2 − {∂_+, ∂_−} to Υ and extends to a homeomorphism S^2 → compactified Υ. Under this homeomorphism, the antipodal map (θ, φ) ↦ (π + θ, π − φ) corresponds to swapping the two ordered bends.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: As φ tends to 0, the two bends X_{θ−φ} and X_{θ+φ} coalesce with the second just ahead of the first in cyclic order, giving ∂_+. As φ tends to π, the ordered pair approaches the other end ∂_−. The formula is bijective on the open cylinder of longitudes and latitudes and has precisely the two limiting ends required for the two-point compactification. Applying the antipodal map gives (π + θ, π − φ), which corresponds to (X_{θ+φ}, X_{θ−φ}) after reducing parameters modulo 2π; this is exactly the coordinate swap.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Suppose {L_t | t ∈ [0, 1]} is a continuous family of oriented lines in R^3 such that L_1 is the same underlying line as L_0 but with the opposite orientation. Then there exist r, s ∈ [0, 1] such that L_r and L_s are perpendicular intersecting lines.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is the same as the proof of Lemma 1.3, but stripped of the surface. For two oriented lines, define g as the dot product of their unit direction vectors and h as the scalar triple product obtained from a vector joining any point on one line to any point on the other line and the two direction vectors. The value h is independent of the chosen points on the two lines. The pair (g, h) detects perpendicular intersecting lines: g = 0 gives perpendicular directions, and h = 0 gives coplanarity, so the nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. The path condition L_1 = L_0 with opposite orientation supplies the same sign reversal used in the Moebius-band argument, yielding an odd map on the two-point compactification of ordered parameter pairs. Borsuk-Ulam, or the equivalent winding argument, gives a zero of (g, h), which is the desired pair.

proof_techniques.name: Bend foliation from developability
proof_techniques.description: The paper obtains a continuous foliation of a smooth embedded paper Moebius band Ω by straight cross-segments. On the locus of nonzero mean curvature, this comes from the kernel of the differential of the Gauss map; zero Gaussian curvature and nonzero mean curvature make this kernel one-dimensional, and its integral curves are straight because the relevant moving frame is constant along them. Flat components of the complement are planar trapezoids or limiting line segments and are filled by interpolating straight cross-segments.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The paper explicitly notes that the later arguments only need injectivity, arc-length preservation, and a continuous bend foliation, not smoothness itself. In the clean-triangulation setting, one possible route is to replace the smooth bend foliation by a cyclic family of straight cross-segments supplied by the triangulation or by an interpolation inside clean triangles.

proof_techniques.name: Centerline parametrization of cross-bends
proof_techniques.description: A crucial preparatory step is proving that every pre-bend intersects the Moebius band centerline exactly once. The proof combines elementary flat-rectangle geometry with a boundary-length contradiction: if a pre-bend met the centerline more than once, its length would exceed λ, but the boundary loop of total length 2λ contains its two image endpoints, forcing total boundary length at least twice the bend length.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original strip quotient by G_β also has a natural centerline. If a family of clean cross-segments can be shown to meet this centerline exactly once, then it can be parametrized cyclically and the paper's T-pattern search can be imitated.

proof_techniques.name: Two-point compactification of ordered pairs
proof_techniques.description: The set of ordered pairs of distinct leaves in a circular family is a cylinder. Adding two ideal points corresponding to the two cyclic collision directions turns it into a 2-sphere. The coordinate swap on ordered pairs becomes the antipodal map on this sphere, especially transparent via the explicit formula (θ, φ) ↦ (X_{θ−φ}, X_{θ+φ}).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a robust way to convert a cyclic one-parameter family of geometric objects into a setting for Borsuk-Ulam. It may apply to any G_β-invariant cyclic family of edges, diagonals, or rulings in the clean triangulation.

proof_techniques.name: Orientation propagation around a Moebius band
proof_techniques.description: For an ordered pair of bends, the proof orients the first bend and transports the orientation continuously along the positive arc of bend parameters to orient the second. Because the band is Moebius, going around the full circle reverses orientation. This is the source of the oddness relation F ∘ Σ = −F for the test map on ordered pairs.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The glide reflection G_β reverses the transverse coordinate y ↦ 1 − y, so it carries the same orientation-reversing Moebius feature. Any line or edge family compatible with the quotient may inherit the sign reversal needed for an odd-map argument.

proof_techniques.name: Dot-product and scalar-triple-product detector for T-patterns
proof_techniques.description: Given two oriented line segments with direction vectors a and b and points or midpoints m_0 and m_1, the paper uses g = a · b and h = (m_0 − m_1) · (a × b). The condition g = 0 means the directions are perpendicular. When g = 0, the condition h = 0 means the two supporting lines are coplanar; nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. Thus common zeros of (g, h) detect perpendicular intersecting supporting lines.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This detector does not depend on smoothness or metric preservation. It can be applied directly to the images of affine edges or selected straight cross-segments in a clean triangulation, provided one can organize them into a suitable oriented family.

proof_techniques.name: Borsuk-Ulam or winding-number zero forcing
proof_techniques.description: The T-pattern proof uses Borsuk-Ulam on an odd map F: S^2 → R^2. The paper also gives a self-contained replacement: if F had no zero, any path from ∂_+ to ∂_− would map to a path in R^2 \ {0} from (1,0) to (−1,0), with a well-defined half-integer winding number; applying the swap involution reverses this winding number while preserving the homotopy class, a contradiction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The winding version may be easier to discretize or adapt to piecewise-linear families arising from clean triangulations, where one might track signs and crossings rather than invoke a smooth Borsuk-Ulam setup.

proof_techniques.name: Cut along one bend of a T-pattern
proof_techniques.description: After finding a T-pattern, the paper cuts the Moebius band along one pre-bend T. The cut-open surface is a bilaterally symmetric trapezoid. The other pre-bend B and the boundary arcs H and D impose a rigid planar bookkeeping structure, while the image T-pattern imposes a spatial triangle whose base is T' and whose height is controlled by B'. This cut turns the global embedded-band problem into elementary trapezoid and triangle inequalities.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original quotient Σ/G_β can likewise be cut along a transverse segment to produce a fundamental polygon. If a T-pattern analogue is found, this technique suggests reducing the lower bound β ≥ √3 to length inequalities among boundary arcs and cross-edges.

proof_techniques.name: Triangle reflection lower bound
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 2.3 proves the key estimate for a triangle with base √(1+t^2) and height at least 1: the sum of the two non-base sides is at least √(5+t^2). The proof reflects one base endpoint across the horizontal line through the apex and applies the triangle inequality. Equality characterizes the isosceles, height-one triangle.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a purely Euclidean estimate and should transfer directly whenever the clean-triangulation geometry produces a triangle with controlled base and height.

proof_techniques.name: Two inequalities balanced by one parameter
proof_techniques.description: The lower bound is reduced to two strict inequalities: δ(t) = √(1+t^2) + √(5+t^2) < 2λ and ν(t) = 2√(5+t^2) − 2t < 2λ. The first is effective when t is large because δ is increasing on (0,∞); the second is effective when t is small because ν is decreasing on R. They balance at t = 1/√3, where both equal 2√3, forcing λ > √3 for every t.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This one-dimensional optimization is likely the sharp numerical core of the original problem as well. A proof strategy can aim to identify the same offset parameter t and derive analogues of the two inequalities from the squeeze-map assumptions.

proof_techniques.name: Stability by vanishing slack
proof_techniques.description: In the triangular-limit theorem, every inequality used in the lower bound becomes asymptotically sharp when λ_n → √3. This forces t_n → 1/√3, the spatial triangle to converge to the equality case of Lemma 2.3, the boundary arcs to become straight with prescribed lengths, and the whole map to converge triangle-by-triangle to the triangular Moebius band. The method is a systematic extraction of equality conditions from the proof of the sharp bound.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original infimum statement, this stability mechanism suggests that any near-realizing clean-triangulated example should resemble the triangular model. It may also guide an explicit upper-bound construction by perturbing or subdividing the triangular limiting configuration.

proof_techniques.name: Abstract oriented-line path lemma
proof_techniques.description: Section 3.2 abstracts the T-pattern argument away from paper surfaces: any continuous path of oriented lines in R^3 that ends at the initial line with opposite orientation contains two perpendicular intersecting lines. The same functions g and h on pairs of parameters provide the obstruction, and the same odd-map argument forces a zero.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This can be used if the clean-triangulation setting naturally provides only a path or cyclic list of oriented supporting lines, rather than a full smooth bend foliation. The lemma isolates exactly the topological orientation-reversal input needed to force a T-pattern.

other_useful_info: At the start of Section 2.1 the author emphasizes that the main proofs only use injectivity, arc-length preservation, and existence of a continuous foliation by bends; smoothness is used only to guarantee that foliation. The triangular Moebius band is described as a limiting object based on a 1 × √3 strip: fold two flaps to make a rhombus and then fold the rhombus in half like a wallet; an equivalent symmetric trapezoid picture has the same T-pattern geometry used in the proof. In the equality-limit picture of Figure 3, the relevant boundary arc lengths are 1/√3, 1/√3, 2/√3, and 2/√3, and the central triangle is equilateral. Section 3.2 notes a dual-number formulation for oriented lines: an oriented line ℓ corresponds to a dual vector ξ_ℓ = a + εb on the Study sphere, where a is its unit direction and b is its moment vector, and ξ_ℓ · ξ_m = 0 exactly when the oriented lines ℓ and m are perpendicular and intersect. The paper also points out that its main theorem and triangular-limit theorem imply a conjecture of Denne-Larsen on folded ribbon Moebius bands; [DL, Corollary 25] had proved the corresponding result under a strong triangular-centerline restriction.
why_relevant: This is the central paper: it proves the sharp sqrt(3) lower bound for smooth embedded paper Möbius bands and identifies the triangular limiting object. Its T-pattern/bend-foliation arguments are the most directly adaptable proof technology for the squeeze-map and clean-triangulation formulation.

title: The Optimal Twisted Paper Cylinder
overall_summary: The paper proves the sharp lower bound λ > 2 for a smooth embedded twisted paper cylinder, where λ is the aspect ratio of the flat cylinder and twisted means that the two boundary components form a nonsplit link. It also proves that examples with λ tending to 2 converge, up to Euclidean isometry, to a degenerate 4-fold wrapping of a right-angled isosceles triangle, and constructs smooth examples for every λ > 2 by smoothing that limiting fold pattern. The lower bound has two proofs: one uses a continuous partition of the developable surface into straight bends, a balanced pair of bends, projection to the plane, and a planar crossing-length estimate; the other uses convex hulls, diameter preservation under convexification, and a bend from one boundary component into the convex hull of the other. For the original problem, the most transferable content is the way topological quotient or linking information is converted into metric inequalities using spanning straight segments, and the way near-equality forces a limiting folded polygonal model. The introduction also explicitly identifies this cylinder theorem as the analogue of Schwartz's optimal paper Möbius band theorem, whose sharp constant is sqrt(3), matching the target constant in the original problem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let λ > 0 and let Γλ be the flat cylinder [0,λ] × [0,1] with (0,y) identified with (λ,y). Let I: Γλ → R^3 be an embedded twisted paper cylinder, meaning a C∞ injective arc-length-preserving map whose two boundary components form a nonsplit link, and let Ω = I(Γλ). Let U ⊂ Ω be the nonempty subset on which Ω has nonzero mean curvature. Then U has a partition into bends, where a bend is a straight line segment lying on Ω and having one endpoint on each component of ∂Ω.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this as a standard result from the classical differential geometry of developable surfaces, specifically Spivak, Corollary 6, p. 241. Since Ω is an isometric image of a flat surface, its Gaussian curvature is zero; on the nonzero mean-curvature locus the surface is ruled by straight line segments. In the cylinder setting, the ruling segments relevant to U extend to the two boundary components, so they are bends. The paper does not reproduce the local differential-geometric proof, but uses this lemma as the local source of bends before extending the partition across flat regions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let λ > 0 and let Γλ = [0,λ] × [0,1]/((0,y) ∼ (λ,y)). Let I: Γλ → R^3 be an embedded twisted paper cylinder and Ω = I(Γλ). A bend is a straight line segment contained in Ω with one endpoint on each of the two boundary components of Ω. Then Ω has a continuous partition into bends.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let U be the nonzero mean-curvature locus of Ω. By Lemma 5.1, U is partitioned into bends. These bends vary continuously because they are disjoint and each has length at least 1, so the partition extends continuously to the closure of U. The complementary components of Ω minus this curved part are flat open trapezoids: two opposite parallel sides lie in ∂Ω, and the other two sides are limiting bends on the boundary of the closure of U. Each such flat trapezoid is then filled by the evident continuous family of straight segments joining its two boundary sides, extending the bend partition to all of Ω. The appendix also notes that for an immersed paper cylinder the analogous statement is that Γλ itself has a partition into prebends; the same proof is applied to the open set of points mapping to nonzero curvature and then to the complementary trapezoids.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Basic bend-length observation (§2.1)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of an embedded paper cylinder I: Γλ → R^3, where Γλ = [0,λ] × [0,1]/((0,y) ∼ (λ,y)) is flat and has width 1, every bend on Ω = I(Γλ) has length at least 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The preimage of a bend is called a prebend, and in the flat cylinder it is a line segment with one endpoint on each boundary component y = 0 and y = 1. Any such flat line segment has Euclidean length at least the vertical separation 1. Since I is arc-length preserving, the image bend has the same length as its prebend, hence length at least 1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let I: Γλ → R^3 be an embedded twisted paper cylinder with image Ω, and assume Ω has the continuous bend partition of Lemma 2.1. A pair (u,v) of distinct bends is called balanced if u and v cut the two boundary components of Ω into four boundary arcs, two on each component, and all four arcs have equal length. Then Ω has a balanced pair of bends.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Identify the midline of Γλ, namely ([0,λ] × {1/2})/∼, with the circle R/λZ. Parametrize bends by the point where their prebends cross this midline: β_t is the bend whose prebend β'_t meets the midline at t. Let s(t) be the slope of β'_t in the flat cylinder. Define f(t) = s(t) − s(t + λ/2). Then f(t + λ/2) = −f(t). By the intermediate value theorem, some t has f(t) = 0, so the prebends β'_t and β'_{t+λ/2} have the same slope. Two parallel line segments in the flat cylinder whose midline crossings differ by λ/2 have endpoints on each boundary component separated by λ/2 along that boundary. Therefore their image bends cut each boundary component into two arcs of length λ/2, giving a balanced pair.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A and B be two line segments in R^2 with lengths ℓ(A) ≥ 1 and ℓ(B) ≥ 1. Let A1,A2 be the endpoints of A and let B1,B2 be the endpoints of B. For j = 1,2, let C_j be a rectifiable arc connecting A_j to B_j. If C_1 ∩ C_2 is nonempty, then ℓ(C_1) + ℓ(C_2) ≥ 2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose x ∈ C_1 ∩ C_2. For j = 1,2, split C_j at x into a subarc C_{j,A} from A_j to x and a subarc C_{j,B} from x to B_j. Then C_{1,A} followed by the reverse of C_{2,A} is a path from A_1 to A_2, so ℓ(C_{1,A}) + ℓ(C_{2,A}) ≥ ℓ(A). Similarly C_{1,B} and C_{2,B} give a path from B_1 to B_2, so ℓ(C_{1,B}) + ℓ(C_{2,B}) ≥ ℓ(B). Adding yields ℓ(C_1)+ℓ(C_2) ≥ ℓ(A)+ℓ(B) ≥ 2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let λ > 0 and Γλ = [0,λ] × [0,1]/((0,y) ∼ (λ,y)). An embedded paper cylinder of aspect ratio λ is a C∞ injective arc-length-preserving map I: Γλ → R^3. It is twisted if the images F and G of the two boundary components of Γλ form a nonsplit link, meaning they cannot be separated by a topological 2-sphere. Theorem 1.1 states that every embedded twisted paper cylinder has λ > 2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first proof uses bends. By Lemma 2.1, Ω = I(Γλ) has a continuous partition into bends, and by Lemma 2.2 it has a balanced pair of bends u,v. Move Ω by a Euclidean isometry so that u lies in a plane identified with R^2 and v lies in a plane parallel to it. Let Π be orthogonal projection to that R^2. Put A = Π(u) and B = Π(v). Since v is parallel to the projection plane and every bend has length at least 1, ℓ(A) = ℓ(u) ≥ 1 and ℓ(B) = ℓ(v) ≥ 1. Let F and G be the boundary loops. Since F and G are linked, Π(F) and Π(G) intersect. Therefore one can choose boundary arcs F* ⊂ F and G* ⊂ G, each joining an endpoint of u to an endpoint of v, so that C_1 = Π(F*) and C_2 = Π(G*) are arcs as in Lemma 2.3 and intersect. Because the pair (u,v) is balanced, ℓ(F*) = ℓ(G*) = λ/2, hence λ = ℓ(F*) + ℓ(G*) ≥ ℓ(C_1)+ℓ(C_2) ≥ 2. The set of aspect ratios achievable by smooth embedded twisted paper cylinders is open, because an example can be fattened slightly, so equality λ = 2 cannot occur; hence λ > 2. The second proof uses convex hulls: by Lemma 3.2, F intersects H = Hull(G). If x ∈ F ∩ H, the bend through x has its other endpoint y on G ⊂ H and has length at least 1, so diam(H) ≥ 1. Lemma 3.1 gives diam(G) = diam(H), and any closed loop of diameter at least 1 has length at least 2. Thus λ = ℓ(G) ≥ 2, and the same openness argument makes the inequality strict.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark after Theorem 1.1: weakened hypotheses
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Ω be an embedded paper cylinder with boundary components F and G. The first proof of Theorem 1.1 works under the weaker hypothesis that, for every linear projection L: R^3 → R^2, the planar curves L(F) and L(G) intersect. The second proof works under the weaker hypothesis that F intersects Hull(G), the convex hull of G. The nonsplit linking hypothesis implies both of these weaker hypotheses.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In the projection proof, linking is used only once: after choosing the projection plane determined by a balanced pair of bends, the proof needs Π(F) ∩ Π(G) ≠ ∅. Thus the stated projection-intersection hypothesis supplies exactly what is needed. In the convex-hull proof, linking is used only through Lemma 3.2 to obtain F ∩ Hull(G) ≠ ∅; if this is assumed directly, the rest of the diameter argument is unchanged. The paper records that nonsplit linking implies both conditions; Lemma 3.2 gives the convex-hull implication explicitly.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any subset S ⊂ R^n, the Euclidean diameter of S is equal to the Euclidean diameter of Hull(S), where Hull(S) denotes the convex hull of S.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The inequality diam(S) ≤ diam(Hull(S)) is immediate from S ⊂ Hull(S). For the reverse, take distinct p,q ∈ H = Hull(S). Let P and Q be the disjoint closed halfspaces bounded by hyperplanes perpendicular to the line pq and respectively containing p and q. If S ∩ P were empty, then R^n − P would be a convex set containing S, forcing H ⊂ R^n − P and contradicting p ∈ H ∩ P. Thus choose p' ∈ S ∩ P. Similarly choose q' ∈ S ∩ Q. By construction, the projection of p'−q' onto the pq direction has length at least ∥p−q∥, so ∥p'−q'∥ ≥ ∥p−q∥. Taking suprema over p,q ∈ H gives diam(S) ≥ diam(H).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let I: Γλ → R^3 be an embedded twisted paper cylinder with image Ω and boundary components F and G. Let H = Hull(G) be the convex hull of G in R^3. Then F ∩ H is nonempty.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume for contradiction that F ∩ H = ∅. Choose p ∈ H. For r ≥ 1 let D_r be dilation by factor r about p, and set F_r = D_r(F). Since H is convex and contains p, H ⊂ D_r(H), and since F is disjoint from H, F_r is disjoint from D_r(H). Because G ⊂ H ⊂ D_r(H), this implies F_r ∩ G = ∅ for all r ≥ 1. For large r, the loop F_r lies outside a sphere that strictly contains G, so F_r and G form a split link. But r ↦ F_r is a smooth isotopy in the complement of G from F to such a split loop. Hence F and G would also be split, contradicting the twisted, nonsplit-link hypothesis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the near-minimal setting λ = 2 + ε with ε → 0 for an embedded twisted paper cylinder I: Γλ → R^3, suppose the preceding argument has produced two bends u and v whose endpoints are labeled so that u joins a to c, v joins b to d, the two boundary loops are nearly unit-length bigons connecting the corresponding endpoints, b is nearly equal to c, and the prebends u' and v' divide Γλ into two nearly unit squares. Let θ be the angle between the vectors a − c and d − b. Then θ is nearly π/2, meaning θ → π/2 as ε → 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper writes s ≲ t to mean max(0,s−t) tends to 0 as ε → 0. Since I preserves arc length and the flat distance ∥a'−d'∥ in one of the nearly unit squares is nearly sqrt(2), the Euclidean distance ∥a−d∥ is at most sqrt(2)+o(1). Using b ≈ c and the facts that the bends have length nearly 1, the law of cosines gives θ ≲ π/2. For the reverse inequality, let V be the ball with v as a diameter; V has diameter nearly 1. For any r with 0 < r ≤ 1, choose a point p on the boundary loop F with ∥c−p∥ ≈ r. The point p is the endpoint of some bend, whose other endpoint q lies on G. Since G is nearly the unit bigon associated to v, q lies in V up to o(1). The bend length estimate gives ∥p−q∥ ≥ 1, so p must lie nearly outside V. In the geometry of the two nearly unit bends this outside-the-diameter-ball condition is equivalent to cos(θ) ≲ r. Because this holds for every r ∈ (0,1), it forces π/2 ≲ θ. Together the two inequalities yield θ ≈ π/2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γλ = [0,λ] × [0,1]/((0,y) ∼ (λ,y)). Suppose I_n: Γ_{λ_n} → R^3 is a sequence of embedded twisted paper cylinders and λ_n → 2. Then, up to ambient Euclidean isometries, I_n converges uniformly to the right-isosceles cylinder map. This limiting map is the common degenerate λ = 2 limit of the folding patterns described in the paper: a continuous piecewise-isometric 4-fold wrapping of the flat cylinder onto a right-angled isosceles triangle.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is presented by taking one cylinder with λ = 2 + ε and letting ε → 0; the words nearly and the notation ≈ mean errors tending to 0 with ε. In the first proof, use the balanced pair of bends u,v from Lemma 2.2 and the projection setup from Theorem 1.1. Since λ ≈ 2 and the inequality chain λ = ℓ(F*)+ℓ(G*) ≥ ℓ(C_1)+ℓ(C_2) ≥ 2 is nearly sharp, the projected arcs C_1,C_2 have lengths nearly equal to the original boundary arcs F*,G*, so projection causes almost no shortening and F* ≈ C_1, G* ≈ C_2. In the proof of Lemma 2.3, choose x ∈ C_1 ∩ C_2. Near equality in the triangle inequalities implies x lies nearly on A = Π(u) and nearly on B = Π(v), and the arcs F*,G* nearly agree with the bigonal paths A_1 to x to B_1 and A_2 to x to B_2. Take a bend whose endpoint on F* is near x; its other endpoint on G is at distance nearly at least 1 from x. Since G is a loop of total length nearly 2 containing this point and nearly containing x, G must be nearly a unit-length bigon with x as one vertex; the same holds for F. Comparing these bigon descriptions with the subarcs F*,G* shows, except for an impossible case of a positive-length overlap of A and B, that x is nearly one endpoint of each of the two bends. Thus F and G are nearly unit bigons connecting endpoints of u and v. The second proof reaches the same summary using convex hulls: let H = Hull(G), choose x ∈ F ∩ H, and let v be the bend from x to y ∈ G. Let P be the plane through x perpendicular to v and let Z be the closed halfspace bounded by P not containing y. Since x ∈ H, some z ∈ G ∩ Z exists. Then ∥y−z∥ ≥ 1, while G has length nearly 2, so G is nearly a bigon with vertices y,z; also ∥y−x∥ ≈ ∥y−z∥ ≈ 1, forcing z ≈ x. The bend through z gives the analogous bigon structure for F. After either start, the common endgame is as follows. Since the bends have length nearly 1, their prebends are nearly perpendicular to ∂Γλ. The two boundary loops of length nearly 2 are split by the prebends into arcs of length nearly 1, so the prebends nearly divide Γλ into two unit squares. Lemma 2.4 shows that the angle between the two bend directions is nearly π/2. The four triangular regions into which the two nearly unit squares are divided therefore have boundaries whose images are nearly those of linear isometries. Hence I is nearly a linear isometry on each of these triangles. This is exactly convergence to the right-isosceles cylinder map.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every ε > 0 there exists an embedded twisted paper cylinder of aspect ratio 2 + ε whose boundary components form a Hopf link.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction smooths the limiting right-isosceles folded model. Using smooth bump functions, make a U-shaped curve U that agrees with parallel line segments at its two ends and joins them smoothly in the middle. The product U × [a,b] is an isometrically embedded rectangle called a pseudofold. Start from one of the λ = 2 limiting folding patterns made of four right isosceles triangular panels, and replace the ideal sharp folds between panels by very narrow pseudofolds. This yields an embedded smooth twisted cylinder whose boundary is not yet exactly totally geodesic. Trim off the rough edges; the result is a smooth embedded twisted paper cylinder with slightly larger aspect ratio. By making the pseudofolds arbitrarily narrow, the final aspect ratio can be made as close to 2 as desired. The boundary components form a Hopf link, as in the illustrated folding model.

proof_techniques.name: Developable-surface bend partition
proof_techniques.description: A smooth isometric embedding of a flat cylinder is developable. On the nonzero mean-curvature locus, classical ruled-surface theory partitions the surface into straight ruling segments. The paper turns these local rulings into global bends joining the two boundary components, then fills the remaining flat trapezoids with parallel straight segments to obtain a continuous bend partition of the whole cylinder. A crucial metric fact is that every bend has length at least the cylinder width, namely 1.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In the original PL problem, every clean triangle has two non-boundary edges joining the two boundary lines of the strip, and squeeze maps increase those edge lengths. These non-boundary edges may serve as a discrete analogue of bends/prebends, with the same lower bound coming from the width of the strip.

proof_techniques.name: Balanced pair from midline slope antisymmetry
proof_techniques.description: Given a continuous bend foliation of the flat cylinder, the paper parametrizes bends by their intersection with the midline circle R/λZ and records the slope s(t) of each prebend. Comparing slopes at antipodal midline points gives f(t) = s(t) − s(t+λ/2), and f(t+λ/2) = −f(t). The intermediate value theorem yields two parallel prebends whose midline points differ by half the circumference; these become a balanced pair cutting each boundary component into equal arcs.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a G_beta-invariant triangulation of the strip, an analogous periodic slope or combinatorial crossing function might select evenly spaced spanning edges or paths. In a Möbius-type quotient, a related balancing argument is a natural place for the constant sqrt(3) to enter, likely through a threefold rather than twofold balanced configuration.

proof_techniques.name: Projection crossing plus planar length estimate
proof_techniques.description: After choosing two bends, the paper places one bend in a plane and the other in a parallel plane, then projects to that plane. Linking of the boundary components forces the projected boundary curves to intersect. A purely planar lemma then says that if two arcs connecting corresponding endpoints of two segments cross, their total length is at least the sum of the segment lengths; since bends have length at least 1, this gives a lower bound on the cylinder circumference.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original map identifies exactly the G_beta-orbits, so after passing to the quotient one may try to extract forced intersections among boundary or edge images. Combined with squeeze inequalities on boundary and spanning edges, a planar crossing estimate could turn those intersections into a lower bound for beta.

proof_techniques.name: Convex hull and diameter lower bound
proof_techniques.description: The second proof replaces the balanced-pair projection argument with convexity. Linking implies that one boundary component F meets the convex hull H of the other component G. A bend from x ∈ F ∩ H to y ∈ G has length at least 1, so H has diameter at least 1. Since a set and its convex hull have the same diameter, G has diameter at least 1, and any closed loop of diameter at least 1 has length at least 2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a way to bypass delicate choices of balanced pairs: prove that a boundary image or orbit representative must enter the convex hull of another, then use long spanning edges to force a diameter and hence a length or period lower bound. It may be adaptable if the quotient induced by G_beta supplies a one-sidedness or non-separation condition weaker than smooth linking.

proof_techniques.name: Near-equality stability of metric inequalities
proof_techniques.description: For λ close to the lower bound, every inequality in the proof must be nearly an equality. The paper repeatedly exploits this: projection nearly preserves length, triangle inequalities in the planar crossing lemma are nearly sharp, boundary arcs become nearly straight bigons, bends become nearly perpendicular to the boundary, and a final angle estimate forces a right angle between two limiting bend directions. The surface is then shown to be nearly a piecewise-linear isometric folding of a right isosceles triangle.
