proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original sqrt(3) infimum, a lower-bound proof may similarly have equality cases corresponding to a triangular Möbius-band limit. Tracking when squeeze inequalities and triangle inequalities are nearly sharp could identify the extremal equilateral-triangle configuration and rule out beta below sqrt(3).

proof_techniques.name: Diameter of convex hull equals diameter of original set
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses a concise halfspace argument: for p,q in Hull(S), choose closed halfspaces perpendicular to pq and containing p and q. Each halfspace must meet S, or else the opposite convex halfspace would contain S and hence Hull(S), contradiction. The two resulting points of S are at least as far apart as p and q.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This elementary convexity tool can convert a point in a convex hull plus a long edge into two actual boundary or vertex images far apart. It is especially useful in PL settings, where convex hulls of polygonal boundary images may be easier to control than the full surface.

proof_techniques.name: Pseudofold smoothing of a limiting fold pattern
proof_techniques.description: To prove sharpness, the paper starts with a degenerate folded polygonal model and replaces ideal creases by narrow smooth U-shaped product regions, called pseudofolds. These pseudofolds interpolate smoothly between flat panels while preserving intrinsic metric locally. Trimming the rough boundary then yields a genuine smooth embedded paper cylinder with aspect ratio arbitrarily close to the limiting value.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the PL original problem, this suggests constructing upper-bound examples by starting from the limiting triangular Möbius/ribbon pattern and perturbing it to create strict squeeze inequalities for every beta above sqrt(3). Even if no smoothing is needed, the idea of inserting small transition strips or subdivisions may provide the necessary slack.

proof_techniques.name: Openness or fattening of achievable aspect ratios
proof_techniques.description: After proving λ ≥ 2, the paper obtains λ > 2 by observing that the set of aspect ratios achieved by smooth embedded twisted paper cylinders is open: any given example can be fattened slightly. This separates the sharp infimum from the nonexistent equality case.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original definition of realized beta uses strict inequalities in the squeeze condition, so it is plausible that realized numbers form an upward-open set or are stable under small perturbations. Establishing such openness would let a construction for beta arbitrarily close to sqrt(3) prove the infimum without realizing the limiting value.

proof_techniques.name: Use only the minimal topological consequence of linking
proof_techniques.description: The paper explicitly notes that the first lower-bound proof needs only that all planar projections of the two boundary curves intersect, while the second needs only that one boundary curve meet the convex hull of the other. Linking is a sufficient but not logically essential hypothesis for those metric arguments.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem does not literally state a linking condition, but the orbit-identification condition may imply some weaker intersection, non-separation, or convex-hull property. The paper indicates that proving such a weaker property may be enough for a metric lower bound.

other_useful_info: The introduction states that this cylinder theorem is the analogue of results in [S1], where Schwartz proved the Halpern-Weaver conjecture: every smooth embedded paper Möbius band has aspect ratio greater than sqrt(3), and minimizing sequences converge to the triangular Möbius band, a 3-fold wrapping of an equilateral triangle. The introduction also cites [S2] for a quantitative near-optimal Möbius-band estimate: if ε < 1/324 and a paper Möbius band has aspect ratio less than sqrt(3)+ε, then it is within 18 sqrt(ε) of an equilateral triangle in Hausdorff distance. The paper relates these smooth objects to folded ribbon knots and says that, with smoothing work along the lines of its pseudofold construction, its cylinder results resolve the n = 1 case of [DL, Conjecture 39]. The appendix remark says the bend-partition lemma has an immersed analogue: for an immersed paper cylinder, the flat domain Γλ is partitioned into prebends, obtained by applying the same nonzero-curvature and flat-trapezoid argument in the domain.
why_relevant: This proves a sharp optimal-aspect-ratio theorem for a closely related quotient surface. It reuses and extends the T-pattern/limiting-folded-polygon machinery from the Möbius-band problem, providing useful analogies for discrete or PL variants.

title: On Nearly Optimal Paper Moebius Bands
overall_summary: The paper proves effective stability for smooth embedded paper Moebius bands whose aspect ratio λ is less than √3+ε: the boundary map is O(√ε)-close to the boundary of the triangular Moebius band, and for ε<1/384 the whole surface is Hausdorff O(√ε)-close to an equilateral triangle of perimeter 2√3. The proof revisits Schwartz's optimality proof for the Halpern-Weaver bound λ>√3: a developable Moebius band has a foliation by straight bends, a Borsuk-Ulam argument forces a perpendicular intersecting pair of bends called a T-pattern, and cutting along one bend gives a trapezoid whose geometry yields the competing lower bounds h(t)=√(1+t²)+t and d(t)=√(5+t²)-t. The new quantitative part consists of perturbation estimates showing that small excess over √3 forces the T-pattern parameter, the relevant triangle, and the boundary curves to be close to their extremal triangular positions; the main analytic device is a sliding trick converting transverse deviation of size √ε into length excess of size ε. For the original problem, the most transferable material is the T-pattern/trapezoid lower-bound mechanism, the slack-to-closeness estimates, and the topological projection/parity argument used to upgrade boundary closeness to Hausdorff closeness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1 (boundary stability)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M_λ be the flat Moebius band obtained by identifying the length-1 sides of a 1×λ rectangle, let I:M_λ→R^3 be a smooth embedded isometric embedding with image Ω, and let I_0:M_√3→R^3 be the triangular Moebius band map. For a map F defined on ∂M_√3, set ||I_0−F||_∞=sup_{p∈∂M_√3} ||I_0(p)−F(p)||. If 0<ε<1/4 and λ<√3+ε, then there is a homeomorphism φ:∂M_√3→∂M_λ and a Euclidean isometry ψ:R^3→R^3 such that ||I_0−ψ∘I∘φ||_∞<6√ε.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first chooses a T-pattern on Ω and normalizes it by a rigid motion so that one bend T′ lies on the X-axis and the other bend B′ lies on the negative Y-axis. Cutting M_λ along the preimage T gives a bilaterally symmetric trapezoid τ with boundary edges H_1,H_2,D_1,D_2 and two copies of T. The quantitative estimates in Section 3.2 show that, after translating so T′ has the same midpoint as the corresponding bend T′_0 in the triangular Moebius band, the four endpoints of the normalized T-pattern are within 3√ε of the corresponding endpoints for I_0; this is Lemma 3.8. Define I_* on ∂τ to be the piecewise affine map agreeing with I at the vertices and affine on each boundary edge. Lemma 3.9 shows that I and I_* differ by less than 3√ε on ∂M_λ; the key input is that any boundary edge with length slack at least ε over its chord would force λ≥√3+ε, while Lemma 2.5 converts such slack into deviation from the affine chord. Let φ:∂M_√3→∂M_λ be the piecewise linear homeomorphism carrying each edge of the triangular-band trapezoid τ_0 to the corresponding edge of τ. Then ||I∘φ−I_*∘φ||_∞<3√ε. Since I_0 and I_*∘φ are affine on corresponding edges of τ_0 and their endpoint values differ by at most 3√ε, convexity along each edge gives ||I_0−I_*∘φ||_∞<3√ε. The triangle inequality gives the stated 6√ε bound, with the earlier normalization absorbed into the isometry ψ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2 (Hausdorff-type stability)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let I:M_λ→R^3 be a smooth embedded paper Moebius band with image Ω, and assume 0<ε<1/4 and λ<√3+ε. Then there is an equilateral triangle ▽_0 of perimeter 2√3 such that every point of Ω is within distance 6√ε of ▽_0. If in addition ε<1/384, then every point of ▽_0 is within distance 18√ε of Ω.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the first assertion, apply Theorem 1.1 to put the boundary ∂Ω within distance d=6√ε of the limiting equilateral triangle ▽_0. By Lemma 3.1, Ω is foliated by straight bends, and the endpoints of every bend lie on ∂Ω. The d-neighborhood N of the convex triangle ▽_0 is convex, so the entire line segment of each bend lies in N once its endpoints lie in N. Since the bends foliate Ω, Ω⊂N. For the second assertion, assume ε<1/384 so d=6√ε<1/3, where 1/3 is the in-radius of ▽_0. Let A be the annular collar around ▽_0 used in Figure 4, with radius-d disks around the vertices, and let C be the solid triangle bounded by the inner boundary of A. Let f:R^3→R^2 be orthogonal projection to the plane of ▽_0. Theorem 1.1 implies f(∂Ω)⊂A. Lemma 3.10 shows that f(∂Ω) represents a generator of π_1(R^2−C), because each boundary edge stays in the corresponding short corridor of A and hence has the same winding behavior as the triangular model. Lemma 3.11 then proves C⊂f(Ω) by contradiction: if p∈C were missed by f(Ω), then f(∂Ω) would generate π_1(R^2−p), but pushing ∂Ω across the bend foliation to twice the core curve would make it represent an even element, impossible for a generator. Thus every vertical line over C meets Ω. Since Ω is already within d of the plane triangle, each point of C is within d of Ω; the paper also notes that every point of ▽_0 is within 2d=12√ε of C. Therefore every point of ▽_0 is within 3d=18√ε of Ω.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1.3 (main Hausdorff corollary)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If 0<ε<1/384 and I:M_λ→R^3 is a smooth embedded paper Moebius band with image Ω and λ<√3+ε, then in the Hausdorff metric Ω is within 18√ε of an equilateral triangle of perimeter 2√3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is an immediate reformulation of Theorem 1.2. The first part of Theorem 1.2 gives that Ω is contained in the 6√ε-neighborhood of the triangle, and the second part gives that the triangle is contained in the 18√ε-neighborhood of Ω. The larger constant 18√ε controls both inclusions, hence bounds the Hausdorff distance.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume 0<ε<1/4. If L<3/2, then √(L²+(13/4)ε)>L+ε.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Square the desired inequality. Since (L+ε)²=L²+(2L+ε)ε and L<3/2 while ε<1/4, one has 2L+ε<3+1/4=13/4. Hence (L+ε)²<L²+(13/4)ε, and both sides are positive, giving the result.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume 0<ε<1/4. If L<3/√2, then √(L²+(9/2)ε)>L+ε/2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Again square the desired inequality. One has (L+ε/2)²=L²+(L+ε/4)ε. The hypotheses imply L+ε/4<3/√2+1/16<9/2, so (L+ε/2)²<L²+(9/2)ε. Taking square roots gives the claimed strict inequality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3 (perturbing an isosceles triangle)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ▽ be a planar triangle with a horizontal base, and let q be its bottom vertex, meaning the vertex not on the base. Let ▽_* be the isosceles triangle with the same base and the same height as ▽, obtained from ▽ by moving q horizontally; write δ=d(▽,▽_*) for this horizontal displacement. Let ∨ be the union of the two non-horizontal sides of ▽ and ∨_* the analogous union for ▽_*. Assume 0<ε<1/4, ℓ(∨_*)<3, and the two sides of ∨_* have slopes whose absolute values exceed 1. If δ≥√(13ε/2), then ℓ(∨)>ℓ(∨_*)+2ε.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let p_1,p_2 be the base vertices of ▽ and let q_* be the bottom vertex of ▽_*. Reflect p_2 across the horizontal line through q and q_* to a point r_2. By symmetry of ▽_*, ℓ(∨_*)=ℓ(p_1r_2), while ℓ(∨)=ℓ(p_1q)+ℓ(qr_2) after reflection. The horizontal distance from q to the line p_1r_2 is at least δ≥√(13ε/2). Since p_1r_2 has slope at least 1 in absolute value, the Euclidean distance from q to that line is at least √(13ε/4). Set L=ℓ(∨_*)/2<3/2. Slide q parallel to the line p_1r_2 to a point q′ that minimizes ℓ(p_1q′)+ℓ(q′r_2); the minimizer makes the triangle (p_1,r_2,q′) isosceles. Thus each of the two new sides has length at least √(L²+(13/4)ε), which is greater than L+ε by Lemma 2.1. Therefore ℓ(∨)≥ℓ(p_1q′)+ℓ(q′r_2)>2L+2ε=ℓ(∨_*)+2ε.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.4 (graph length comparison)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S be a line segment with ℓ(S)<3. Let I:S→R^3 be a unit speed map, let I_*:S→R^3 be the affine map agreeing with I at the endpoints of S, set S′=I(S) and S′_*=I_*(S), and form the graphs Γ={(x,I(x)):x∈S} and Γ_*={(x,I_*(x)):x∈S} in R×R^3 after identifying S with an interval in R. Then ℓ(Γ_*)≤ℓ(Γ)≤3√2, and ℓ(Γ)−ℓ(Γ_*)≤ℓ(S′)−ℓ(S′_*).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because I is unit speed and the domain length equals ℓ(S′), the graph Γ has constant speed √2, so ℓ(Γ)=√(ℓ(S′)²+ℓ(S′)²)<3√2. The affine map I_* has speed s=ℓ(S′_*)/ℓ(S′), so Γ_* has speed √(1+s²) and ℓ(Γ_*)=√(ℓ(S′)²+ℓ(S′_*)²), which is at most ℓ(Γ) and less than 3√2. For the deficit inequality, fix a=ℓ(S′)² and consider f(t)=√(a+t²). Since |f′(t)|≤1 for t≥0, f(ℓ(S′))−f(ℓ(S′_*))≤ℓ(S′)−ℓ(S′_*). This is exactly ℓ(Γ)−ℓ(Γ_*)≤ℓ(S′)−ℓ(S′_*).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.5 (deviation from chord forces length slack)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of Lemma 2.4, assume 0<ε<1/4. If sup_{p∈S} ||I(p)−I_*(p)||≥3√ε, then ℓ(S′)>ℓ(S′_*)+ε.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose p∈S with ||I(p)−I_*(p)||≥3√ε, and let q=(p,I(p))∈Γ. The point of Γ_* with the same R-coordinate is at least 3√ε away from q in the R^3 direction. Since the affine graph Γ_* has slope at most 1, the actual distance from q to Γ_* in R^4 is at least 3√ε/√2=√(9ε/2). Let L=ℓ(Γ_*)/2; by Lemma 2.4, L<3/√2. Applying the same sliding trick as in Lemma 2.3 to a curve with endpoints Γ_* and a point at distance √(9ε/2) from Γ_* gives ℓ(Γ)−ℓ(Γ_*)≥2√(L²+(9/2)ε)−2L. Lemma 2.2 makes the right side greater than ε. Lemma 2.4 gives ℓ(S′)−ℓ(S′_*)≥ℓ(Γ)−ℓ(Γ_*), so ℓ(S′)>ℓ(S′_*)+ε.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1 (bend foliation)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every smooth embedded paper Moebius band Ω has a foliation by bends, where a bend is a straight line segment contained in Ω whose endpoints, and only its endpoints, lie in ∂Ω.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper gives a sketch and cites standard developable-surface arguments. On the open set where the mean curvature is nonzero, there is a unique tangent direction in which the differential of the Gauss map is trivial. These directions integrate to a foliation by line segments. The complementary pieces are planar trapezoids, and the line-segment foliation extends over them in the evident way. The cited references cover common cases, and [S0, Prop. 2.1] is cited for the general case.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2 (existence of a T-pattern)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every smooth embedded paper Moebius band Ω has a T-pattern, meaning a pair of disjoint bends in Ω whose supporting lines in R^3 intersect and are perpendicular.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Using Lemma 3.1, the space of bends in the bend foliation is homeomorphic to a circle. The space of ordered pairs of unequal bends has a two-point compactification homeomorphic to S^2. Using the dot product and cross product of the data associated to the two bends, the paper defines two odd real-valued functions on this S^2. Simultaneous vanishing of these two functions is precisely the condition that the two supporting lines are perpendicular and intersect, while the bends themselves are disjoint. By the Borsuk-Ulam theorem, two odd functions on S^2 must have a common zero, giving the desired T-pattern.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Recalled Lemma G / optimality inequality λ>√3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let I:M_λ→R^3 be a smooth embedded paper Moebius band with image Ω. Choose a T-pattern and rotate Ω so that one bend T′ lies on the X-axis and the other bend B′ lies on the negative Y-axis. Let T=I^{-1}(T′), cut M_λ open along T to get the bilaterally symmetric trapezoid τ used in Figure 3, and let t be the horizontal displacement of T in τ. Let H and D be the two boundary arcs determined by this cut, with images H′ and D′, so that λ=ℓ(H)+t=ℓ(D)−t. Then ℓ(H)=ℓ(H′)>ℓ(T′)=√(1+t²), and ℓ(D)=ℓ(D′)≥ℓ(∨)>√(5+t²), where ∨ is the union of the two non-horizontal sides of the planar triangle associated to the T-pattern. Consequently λ>max(h(t),d(t)), where h(t)=√(1+t²)+t and d(t)=√(5+t²)−t. Since h is increasing, d is decreasing, and h(1/√3)=d(1/√3)=√3, every smooth embedded paper Moebius band satisfies λ>√3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The T-pattern normalization makes T′ a horizontal segment and B′ a perpendicular segment meeting its supporting line. In the cut-open trapezoid, the bend T has vertical displacement 1 and horizontal displacement t, so its length and the length of T′ are √(1+t²). The boundary arc H′ connects the endpoints corresponding to T′ and is longer than the chord T′, giving ℓ(H)>√(1+t²). The other boundary arc D′ must contain enough length to span the two non-horizontal sides ∨ of the triangle whose base is T′ and whose height is greater than or equal to the length of B, at least 1; the elementary triangle estimate gives ℓ(∨)>√(5+t²). Combining these with λ=ℓ(H)+t=ℓ(D)−t gives λ>h(t) and λ>d(t). The functions h and d meet at t_0=1/√3 with value √3; h is increasing and d is decreasing, so if t≥t_0 then h(t)≥√3 and if t≤t_0 then d(t)≥√3. The strict inequalities yield λ>√3 in all cases.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3 (control of the T-pattern displacement)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let I:M_λ→R^3 be a smooth embedded paper Moebius band with image Ω, assume 0<ε<1/4 and λ<√3+ε, and use the normalized T-pattern notation of the optimality inequality: t is the horizontal displacement of the bend T in the cut trapezoid, and t_0=1/√3 is the corresponding value for the triangular Moebius band. Then |t−t_0|<4ε/3. In particular t∈(0,1).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the functions h(t)=√(1+t²)+t and d(t)=√(5+t²)−t from the optimality proof. Both are convex in the relevant sense used in the paper, h′(t_0)=3/2, and d′(t_0)=−3/4. The inequality λ<√3+ε together with λ>h(t) implies that if t≥t_0 then h(t)−h(t_0)<ε, while convexity and h′(t_0)=3/2 give h(t)−h(t_0)≥(3/2)(t−t_0). Thus t−t_0<2ε/3. Similarly, if t≤t_0 then d(t)−d(t_0)<ε and convexity gives d(t)−d(t_0)≥(3/4)(t_0−t), hence t_0−t<4ε/3. The paper records the uniform bound |t−t_0|<4ε/3. Since ε≤1/4 and 1/3<t_0<2/3, this implies t∈(0,1).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4 (length bound)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setup of Lemma 3.3, let H and D be the two boundary arcs in the cut-open trapezoid satisfying λ=ℓ(H)+t=ℓ(D)−t. Then ℓ(H)<ℓ(D)<3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 3.3 gives t>0, so ℓ(H)=λ−t and ℓ(D)=λ+t, hence ℓ(H)<ℓ(D). Since ε≤1/4, the hypothesis λ<√3+ε implies λ<2. Lemma 3.3 also gives t<1. Therefore ℓ(D)=λ+t<2+1=3.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5 (length of the horizontal T-bend)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setup of Lemma 3.3, let T′ be the normalized bend lying on the X-axis. Then |ℓ(T′)−2t_0|<ε, where t_0=1/√3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The length of T′ is f(t)=√(1+t²), because the preimage bend T has vertical displacement 1 and horizontal displacement t. At the triangular value t_0=1/√3, f(t_0)=2t_0. Lemma 3.3 gives t,t_0∈(0,1) and |t−t_0|<4ε/3. On (0,1), |f′(s)|=s/√(1+s²)<3/4. Hence |ℓ(T′)−2t_0|=|f(t)−f(t_0)|<(3/4)(4ε/3)=ε.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.6 (height bound for the T-pattern triangle)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setup of Lemma 3.3, let ▽ be the planar triangle associated to the T-pattern in Figure 3, with base T′ and height y. Then y<1+ε.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a triangle of height y, the lower-bound argument leading to d(t)=√(5+t²)−t generalizes to d(y,t)=√(1+t²+4y²)−t. For fixed y this function decreases in t. The generalized minimax argument gives λ>h(t_y), where t_y solves h(t_y)=d(y,t_y). Solving yields t_y=±y²/√(1+2y²). The negative choice would force λ>√5, which is impossible under λ<√3+ε with ε<1/4. The positive choice gives λ>g(y)=√(1+2y²). Now g(1)=√3 and g′(y)>1 for y≥1. If y≥1+ε, then g(y)>√3+ε, contradicting λ<√3+ε; if y<1 the conclusion is immediate. Hence y<1+ε.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.7 (near-isosceles bound for the T-pattern triangle)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setup of Lemma 3.3, let ▽ be the T-pattern triangle, let ▽_* be the isosceles triangle with the same horizontal base and the same height as ▽, and let δ=d(▽,▽_*) be the horizontal displacement needed to move the bottom vertex of ▽ to the bottom vertex of ▽_*. Then δ<√(13ε/2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume for contradiction that δ≥√(13ε/2). Let ∨ and ∨_* be the unions of the two non-horizontal sides of ▽ and ▽_* respectively. By the length bound Lemma 3.4, ℓ(∨_*)≤ℓ(∨)≤ℓ(D′)=ℓ(D)<3. The base of ▽ has length √(1+t²)<2 by Lemma 3.3, and its height is at least 1, so the two sides of ∨_* have slopes exceeding 1 in absolute value. Lemma 2.3 applies and yields ℓ(∨)−ℓ(∨_*)>2ε, hence the perimeter of ▽ exceeds the perimeter of ▽_* by more than 2ε. If t≥t_0, the isosceles comparison triangle ▽_* has perimeter at least 2√3, and the boundary lengths give 2λ=ℓ(H)+ℓ(D)=ℓ(H′)+ℓ(D′)≥perimeter(▽)>2√3+2ε, contradicting λ<√3+ε. If t<t_0, then λ≥ℓ(∨)−t>ℓ(∨_*)−t+2ε>d(t)+2ε≥√3+2ε, again contradicting λ<√3+ε. Therefore δ<√(13ε/2).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.8 (T-pattern endpoint bound)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setup of Lemma 3.3, let (T′,B′) be the normalized T-pattern of Ω and let (T′_0,B′_0) be the corresponding T-pattern of the triangular Moebius band. Translate Ω so that T′ and T′_0 have the same midpoint. Then each endpoint of T′ and B′ is within distance 3√ε of the corresponding endpoint of T′_0 and B′_0; in fact the endpoints of T′ are within ε/2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 3.5 says |ℓ(T′)−ℓ(T′_0)|=|ℓ(T′)−2t_0|<ε. Since T′ and T′_0 are collinear with the same midpoint after translation, each endpoint of T′ is within ε/2 of the corresponding endpoint of T′_0. For the endpoints of B′, Lemma 3.6 says the height of the T-pattern triangle differs from the triangular model height by less than ε, so the relevant y-coordinates differ by at most ε. Lemma 3.7 says the horizontal displacement from the isosceles position is less than √(13ε/2), giving the same bound for the x-coordinate error. Thus the Euclidean distance from each endpoint of B′ to its model endpoint is less than √((13/2)ε+ε²), which is less than 3√ε for ε∈(0,1/4).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.9 (boundary curve close to its affine chord map)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let I:M_λ→R^3 be as in Lemma 3.3, cut open along the normalized bend T to obtain the trapezoid τ, and regard ∂τ as having six edges H_1,H_2,D_1,D_2,T_1,T_2, where T_1,T_2 are the two copies of T. Let I_*:∂τ→R^3 be the piecewise affine map agreeing with I at the vertices and affine on each edge; this induces a piecewise linear map on ∂M_λ. Then sup_{p∈∂M_λ} ||I(p)−I_*(p)||<3√ε.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each boundary edge S among H_1,H_2,D_1,D_2, Lemma 3.4 implies ℓ(S)<3. The paper then uses the near-optimality λ<√3+ε to assert that no one of these boundary curves can have more than ε of length slack over its chord, i.e. ℓ(I(S))<ℓ(I_*(S))+ε; otherwise the optimality inequalities would gain at least ε and contradict λ<√3+ε. Applying Lemma 2.5 contrapositive to each such S shows sup_{p∈S} ||I(p)−I_*(p)||<3√ε. On the cut edges T_1,T_2, the image is already the straight bend T′, so the affine map agrees there. Taking the maximum over the boundary gives the stated sup-norm estimate on ∂M_λ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.10 (winding of the projected boundary)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume the setup for the second part of Theorem 1.2: ε<1/384, d=6√ε<1/3, ▽_0 is the equilateral triangle of perimeter 2√3 supplied by Theorem 1.1, A is the annular collar around ▽_0 shown in Figure 4 with radius-d disks around the vertices, C is the solid triangle bounded by the inner boundary of A, and f:R^3→R^2 is projection to the plane of ▽_0. Then the loop f(∂Ω) represents a generator of π_1(R^2−C).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 1.1 places the relevant boundary arcs of Ω inside the corresponding portions of the annular collar A. For example, the image H′_1 connects a point in the disk D_{x′} around one vertex to a point in the disk D_{u′} around the adjacent vertex and remains in the short corridor of A between those disks; because d<1/3, this corridor structure is topologically separated from the other way around C, so H′_1 cannot wind around the opposite side. The same analysis applies to H′_2,D′_1,D′_2. Therefore f(∂Ω) follows the same cyclic route around C as the boundary of the triangular model f(∂Ω_0). That model loop is a generator of π_1(R^2−C), so f(∂Ω) is also a generator.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.11 (projection covers the inner triangle)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setup of Lemma 3.10, C⊂f(Ω).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Suppose p∈C is not in f(Ω). Then f(Ω)⊂R^2−p, and by Lemma 3.10 the loop f(∂Ω) generates π_1(R^2−p)≅Z. Let Λ be the core curve of Ω, defined as the curve through the midpoints of the bends in the bend foliation. Pushing the boundary ∂Ω inward along the bends gives a homotopy, avoiding p because p∉f(Ω), from f(∂Ω) to a curve denoted f(2Λ), which winds twice around f(Λ). Thus f(2Λ) would also have to be a generator of π_1(R^2−p). But f(2Λ) represents an even element of the infinite cyclic group π_1(R^2−p), so it cannot be a generator. This contradiction proves every p∈C lies in f(Ω).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.5 (sharpness construction)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper constructs polygonal paper Moebius bands, smoothable by the Halpern-Weaver pseudo-fold method, whose aspect ratios are √3+O(ε) while their images contain points at distance O(√ε) from the limiting equilateral triangle. Thus the O(√ε) scale in Theorems 1.1 and 1.2 is sharp in order of magnitude.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start with M_√3 and insert a vertical strip B of width O(ε). In the triangular model, slit the equilateral triangle ▽_0 along its vertical midline, obtaining two triangular doors. Rotate the doors about their outer b-sides by an angle O(√ε). Their vertical sides form a sharp V-shaped crack whose width is O(ε) but whose height out of the plane is O(√ε), reflecting 1−cos θ=O(θ²) and sin θ=O(θ). Fold the inserted strip B so its two vertical edges form an isometric V-shaped plug. Rotate the plug by π about the Z-axis, tilt it slightly, and glue it to the crack along the matching a-sides. Replacing the corresponding triangular piece of the triangular Moebius band by this wrinkle gives a polygonal paper Moebius band with only O(ε) increase in aspect ratio but O(√ε) Hausdorff deviation. The paper says standard pseudo-fold smoothing from Halpern-Weaver approximates these polygonal examples by smooth embedded paper Moebius bands.

proof_techniques.name: Borsuk-Ulam forcing of a T-pattern
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the bend foliation of a smooth developable Moebius band to parameterize bends by a circle. Ordered unequal pairs of bends compactify to a sphere S^2. Two odd functions on this sphere, built from dot-product and cross-product data of the two bends, vanish simultaneously exactly when the two supporting lines are perpendicular and intersect. Borsuk-Ulam supplies a common zero, producing a T-pattern.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the clean-triangulated quotient of the strip, one can look for an analogue of a bend family or a combinatorial family of chords joining boundary points. A topological odd-map or parity argument forcing a perpendicular/intersecting pair could recreate the T-pattern input needed for a √3 lower bound.

proof_techniques.name: Cut-open trapezoid and competing one-variable lower bounds
proof_techniques.description: After a T-pattern is found, the band is cut open along one bend to form a symmetric trapezoid. A single horizontal displacement parameter t controls the length of the cut bend, √(1+t²), and the opposite T-pattern geometry forces two lower bounds on the aspect ratio: h(t)=√(1+t²)+t and d(t)=√(5+t²)−t. These functions balance at t=1/√3, where both equal √3; monotonicity on either side gives λ>√3.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the most direct template for proving the lower bound β≥√3. In the original problem, the squeeze inequalities on triangle edges may supply analogues of the chord-length inequalities ℓ(H)>√(1+t²) and ℓ(D)>√(5+t²).

proof_techniques.name: Length slack converts to √ε geometric deviation
proof_techniques.description: Lemmas 2.4 and 2.5 lift a curve and its chord to graphs in one higher dimension. If the curve deviates from its affine chord by at least 3√ε, then a point of the graph is at distance at least √(9ε/2) from the chord graph. A sliding argument shows any such detour increases graph length by at least ε, and the graph deficit is bounded above by the original curve's length slack over its chord.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This can be used whenever a near-optimal configuration has only O(ε) total length slack. For a PL or affine-on-triangles map in the original problem, it suggests proving that boundary images or important polygonal arcs must lie within O(√ε) of straight chords.

proof_techniques.name: Sliding trick for triangle perturbations
proof_techniques.description: In Lemma 2.3, a non-isosceles triangle with fixed horizontal base and height is compared to the isosceles one by reflecting one base endpoint and sliding the bottom vertex parallel to the reflected chord until the broken path is minimized. A transverse displacement δ from the isosceles position contributes quadratically to the two-side length, so δ≈√ε is the threshold for an ε length increase.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Near the critical value √3, any forced triangle in the image should become nearly equilateral or nearly isosceles. This lemma gives a quantitative way to turn failure of symmetry into an exploitable length surplus.

proof_techniques.name: Derivative stability at the minimax point
proof_techniques.description: The functions h(t)=√(1+t²)+t and d(t)=√(5+t²)−t not only prove the sharp lower bound but also control near-equality. Since h′(1/√3)=3/2 and d′(1/√3)=−3/4, the condition λ<√3+ε forces |t−1/√3|<4ε/3. This is a simple but effective convexity argument: away from the balancing parameter, one of the lower bounds rises linearly.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original problem yields the same or analogous lower-bound functions, this gives an immediate robust contradiction for β<√3 and a compactness description of near-realizers with β≈√3.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary control plus convex ruled filling
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 1.2 upgrades boundary closeness to whole-surface closeness by using the bend foliation. Once ∂Ω lies in a tubular neighborhood N of a convex triangle, every bend has endpoints in N and hence lies in N because N is convex. Since the bends foliate Ω, the entire band lies in N.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A clean triangulation consists of affine triangular pieces with vertices on the boundary of the strip. If boundary vertices or boundary arcs can be controlled near a convex triangular set, convexity may similarly force the images of entire triangles to remain near that set.

proof_techniques.name: Projection winding and even-core parity obstruction
proof_techniques.description: For the reverse Hausdorff inclusion, the paper projects Ω to the plane of the limiting triangle. Boundary closeness places the projected boundary in an annular collar and fixes its winding number around an inner triangle C. If a point p∈C were missed by the projection, the projected boundary would generate π_1(R^2−p); but pushing the boundary through the bend foliation to the core curve makes it homotopic to twice the core, an even element, contradicting generation of Z.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The quotient of the strip by the flip-translation is a Moebius band, so the boundary-to-twice-core parity phenomenon is intrinsic. This argument may help rule out degenerate near-critical maps or prove that a projected image must cover a triangular region.

proof_techniques.name: Sharp wrinkle construction
proof_techniques.description: The paper's sharpness example inserts an O(ε)-width strip and folds it into a V-shaped plug matching a V-shaped crack made by rotating two triangular doors by O(√ε). This changes aspect ratio only by O(ε) but creates O(√ε) out-of-plane deviation, showing that the stability exponent cannot generally be improved.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For an infimum statement, this construction is a reminder that near-critical realizations may have √ε-scale geometric wrinkles even when the parameter excess is ε. It may also inspire explicit PL constructions of realized β just above √3.

other_useful_info: The paper is an effective version of results from [S0], which proved the optimal strict bound λ>√3 for smooth embedded paper Moebius bands and a qualitative convergence theorem to the triangular Moebius band as λ→√3. The bend foliation is attributed to standard developable-surface results, with references [CL], [HN] as listed in the bibliography, [Mas], and [S0, Prop. 2.1]; the T-pattern and optimality inequalities are cited as [S0, Lemma T] and [S0, Lemma G]. The smoothing of polygonal sharpness examples uses the pseudo-fold method of Halpern-Weaver [HW]. The author remarks that Theorem 1.1 controls only the boundary map and suggests whole-map control should be possible with more effort. The cutoff ε<1/384 is used to ensure 6√ε is smaller than the in-radius 1/3 of the limiting equilateral triangle, so that the annular collar in the projection argument has the intended topology.
why_relevant: This gives an effective quantitative stability version of the optimal-paper-Möbius theorem. The estimates controlling bands with aspect ratio close to sqrt(3) are useful for turning an infimum statement into a robust contradiction or convergence-to-triangle argument.

title: Geometric Realization of Möbius Triangulations
why_relevant: This paper studies when triangulations of the Möbius band can be realized as embedded flat-faced polyhedral surfaces in R^3. Its PL realization techniques are directly relevant to clean triangulations and affine-on-triangles maps.

title: Über ein abwickelbares Möbiusband
why_relevant: Wunderlich’s paper is a key classical source on finite-width developable Möbius bands. It refines Sadowsky’s framework and supplies constructions and geometric constraints that inform the existence side and the developable-surface intuition.

title: Closed Unstretchable Knotless Ribbons and the Wunderlich Functional
overall_summary: The paper gives a rigorous kinematic and variational formulation for closed unstretchable ribbons obtained by isometric deformation of a flat rectangular strip, including both orientable bands and nonorientable Möbius-type bands. Its core technique is to represent the reference strip and the deformed ribbon by compatible ruled parametrizations with a directrix and a generatrix, then translate isometry, developability, edge-gluing, twist, and local injectivity into explicit differential constraints on two vector-valued functions d and g. It proves that the bending energy of such a ribbon dimensionally reduces to the Wunderlich functional and derives constrained Euler-Lagrange equations with matching and jump conditions. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the precise treatment of a strip quotient with reversed boundary identification, the ruled/directrix parametrization, the local non-overlap inequality, and the distinction between local injectivity and global injectivity in ribbon models.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Closed-ribbon edge conditions and orientability, Sect. 2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let D be the rectangular strip [0,ℓ] × [0,2b] × {0} in Euclidean 3-space E, with coordinates x1,x2,x3 and orthonormal basis i1,i2,i3. Let χ be an isometric deformation of D, assumed C2 on the interior of D, and let S = χ(D) be a C1 surface. If χ(0,x2,0) = χ(ℓ,x2,0) for every x2 in [0,2b], then S is an orientable closed ribbon and its boundary has two disjoint edges. If χ(0,x2,0) = χ(ℓ,2b − x2,0) for every x2 in [0,2b], then S is a nonorientable closed ribbon and its boundary has one edge. The paper allows any number of twists before imposing either edge identification.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the paper's basic kinematic setup. The first condition identifies the two short edges of the rectangle with the same transverse coordinate and produces an annulus-type closed ribbon; the second identifies the two short edges with the transverse coordinate reversed and produces a Möbius-type nonorientable ribbon. The boundary-component count follows from whether the two long edges of the rectangle are glued to themselves separately or are interchanged by the reversal. These conditions are later converted into endpoint conditions on the directrix d and the generatrix g.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Ruled-surface representation of isometric ribbon deformations, Sect. 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For an isometric deformation χ of the rectangular strip D into a closed ribbon S as above, the Gaussian curvature of S vanishes. The subset of S where its mean curvature H is nonzero is covered by straight line segments, called rulings, which are asymptotic curves. Connected components where H = 0 are flat pieces of trapezoidal, triangular, or isolated line-segment type and can be ruled compatibly with adjacent curved portions. Consequently S can be represented as a ruled surface. The rulings of S pull back under χ to rulings of D, and each ruling of S intersects the boundary ∂S in exactly two points.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The vanishing of Gaussian curvature is a consequence of isometry from a planar strip. The paper invokes the theorem of Hartman and Nirenberg that a surface region with zero Gaussian curvature and nonzero mean curvature is ruled by straight asymptotic curves. Flat components have the simple polygonal forms inherited from the rectangular reference strip and can be filled with straight rulings that match continuously to adjacent curved rulings. Since χ is an isometry, a ruling in S corresponds to a straight ruling segment in D. The connecting edge conditions prevent a single ruling of D from spanning the entire strip length under the end identification, so each ruling segment has exactly two boundary endpoints.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Boundary smoothness versus ruling intersections, Sect. 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let χ be C2 on the interior of D. At a boundary point x° of D through which nearby rulings do not intersect, χ extends as a C2 isometric deformation to a small two-dimensional neighborhood of x° in the plane of D. In contrast, if a family of rulings intersects at a boundary point, χ need not be C1 there; a curved patch covered by rulings meeting at one boundary point has different limiting normals along different rulings unless that patch is flat.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If no nearby rulings intersect at x°, the ruling directions vary continuously and the ruling segments can be extended slightly outside the rectangle without crossing. Since an isometric deformation maps each ruling rigidly, χ has a natural C2 extension to a neighborhood of x° in the plane. For the failure case, the paper parameterizes a curved patch Tc whose rulings meet at p by r̃(φ,ρ) = p + ρ g(φ), where ρ is distance along a ruling and φ labels the ruling. Its normal n = r̃ρ × r̃φ divided by its norm is independent of ρ along each ruling. If two rulings have different normals n1 and n2, continuity of the normal at p would force n1 = n2, contradicting curvature of the patch. Thus C1 boundary regularity can fail at a nonflat ruling fan.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Directrix and generatrix parametrization with metric constraints, Sect. 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the orientable case the reference directrix Cr is parametrized by arclength c(α) = (2b − α)i2 for α in [0,b), c(α) = (α − b)i1 + b i2 for α in [b,ℓ+b), and c(α) = ℓ i1 + (ℓ + 2b − α)i2 for α in [ℓ+b,ℓ+2b]. In the nonorientable case the first branch is instead c(α) = α i2 for α in [0,b), while the middle and last branches are the same. Let d(α) = χ(c(α)) be the spatial directrix. For each α, let f(α) be the unit reference ruling direction through c(α), chosen so that (c′(α) × f(α)) · i3 > 0, let θ(α) be the angle between c′(α) and f(α), and let g(α) be the unit spatial ruling direction. Then f = cos θ c′ + sin θ i3 × c′. There are endpoint functions β_− and β_+ such that P = {(α,β): α in [0,ℓ+2b], β in [β_−(α),β_+(α)]} parametrizes the strip and ribbon by x̂(α,β) = c(α) + β f(α) and r̂(α,β) = d(α) + β g(α). The deformation is determined by r = χ(x) if and only if x = x̂(α,β) and r = r̂(α,β). The following conditions are necessary and sufficient for these ruled parametrizations to define an isometric deformation: |c′| = |d′| = 1, |f| = |g| = 1, |f′| = |g′|, c′ · f = d′ · g, and c′ · f′ = d′ · g′.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The reference directrix is chosen so that it intersects every ruling exactly once; it consists of the midline of the rectangle plus two short-edge segments, with the first short-edge segment chosen differently in the orientable and nonorientable cases. The functions β_− and β_+ are the signed distances from the directrix point c(α) to the two boundary endpoints of the ruling through c(α). Since χ maps rulings rigidly, the deformed point corresponding to c(α)+β f(α) has the form d(α)+β g(α). The isometry constraints are obtained by comparing the first fundamental forms of x̂ and r̂: x̂_α = c′+β f′, x̂_β = f, r̂_α = d′+β g′, and r̂_β = g. Equality of the metric coefficients for every β is equivalent to equality of the constant, linear, and quadratic coefficients in β, giving exactly the displayed conditions. The paper cites Chen, Fosdick, and Fried for the necessary-and-sufficient formulation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Darboux-frame consequences of isometry and developability, Sect. 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let t = d′ and define n = (t × g)/|t × g| and m = n × t along the spatial directrix. Since c consists of straight-line segments and χ is isometric, d is a union of geodesics, so its geodesic curvature κ_g vanishes except possibly at α = b and α = ℓ+b. The generatrix has the form g = cos θ t + sin θ m. If κ_n is the normal curvature and τ_g is the geodesic torsion of d, then the isometry constraints imply d″ · g = 0 and κ_n cos θ + τ_g sin θ = 0. Consequently g′ = −θ′(sin θ t − cos θ m), m′ = −κ_n cot θ n, and n′ = −κ_n(t − cot θ m).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Differentiating c′ · f = d′ · g and using c″ = 0 together with c′ · f′ = d′ · g′ gives d″ · g = 0. The Darboux equations for the frame {t,m,n} are t′ = κ_g m + κ_n n, m′ = −κ_g t + τ_g n, and n′ = −κ_n t − τ_g m. Since the reference directrix branches are straight geodesics and isometries preserve geodesics, κ_g = 0 away from the corners of the directrix. Substituting g = cos θ t + sin θ m into g′ and comparing with the equality |f′| = |g′| forces the normal component κ_n cos θ + τ_g sin θ to vanish, which is the developability relation in this parametrization. With this normal component removed, the stated formulas for g′, m′, and n′ follow directly from the Darboux equations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Gluing maps and reduction of integrals to the midline, Sect. 2.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let α_L be the largest α in [b,ℓ+b] such that the ruling through c(α) intersects the left short edge of D, and let α_R be the smallest α in [b,ℓ+b] such that the ruling through c(α) intersects the right short edge of D. Let I = [b,α_L] ∪ [α_R,ℓ+b] and K = [0,b] ∪ [ℓ+b,ℓ+2b]. The rulings crossing the identified short edges define a bijection α̂:I→K. In the orientable case, α̂(α) = b + ℓ + (α−b) tan θ(α) on [b,α_L] and α̂(α) = b + (α−b−ℓ) tan θ(α) on [α_R,ℓ+b]. In the nonorientable case, α̂(α) = b + (α−ℓ−b) tan θ(α) on [b,α_L] and α̂(α) = ℓ + b + (b−α) tan θ(α) on [α_R,ℓ+b]. The corresponding extensions of r̂ across the seam imply that for every integrable function φ:S→R, ∫_{0}^{ℓ+2b} ∫_{β_−(α)}^{β_+(α)} (φ∘r̂)|r̂_α × r̂_β| dβ dα = ∫_{b}^{ℓ+b} ∫_{−b csc θ(α)}^{b csc θ(α)} (φ∘r̂)|r̂_α × r̂_β| dβ dα.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For rulings that meet the glued short edges, the paper writes explicit identities equating the two parametrizations of the same deformed ruling segment. In the orientable case the β-coordinate is translated along the same ruling direction; in the nonorientable case it is reversed because the transverse coordinate is glued with opposite orientation. Differentiating these identities gives the corresponding equality of area Jacobians, with a factor |α̂′(α)|. A change of variables from (α̂(α),β) to the corresponding midline parameters transfers the integrals over the short-edge parts of the directrix to integrals over the missing portions of the midline directrix. Combining these two transferred pieces with the middle interval [α_L,α_R], where the full ruling width is already β in [−b csc θ,b csc θ], yields the displayed integral identity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: No-overlap inequality for rulings, Sect. 2.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume the rulings of the ribbon do not intersect in the interior. For θ equal to the angle between the midline tangent d′ and the generatrix g, the paper derives the necessary pointwise inequalities 0 ≤ sin² θ − b θ′ and 0 ≤ sin² θ + b θ′, equivalently sin⁴ θ − b² θ′² ≥ 0. In the later construction this same inequality is used to guarantee local injectivity of the ruled parametrization in the interior.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose α1 ≤ α2 in [b,ℓ+b]. The boundary points reached from d(α_i) by following the ruling in the +g direction lie on geodesics orthogonal to the midline that meet the midline at parameters α_i + b cot θ(α_i). If rulings do not intersect in the ribbon, these intercept parameters must be ordered, so α1 + b cot θ(α1) ≤ α2 + b cot θ(α2). Dividing the difference by α2−α1 and taking α2→α1 gives 0 ≤ 1 − b csc² θ θ′, which is equivalent to 0 ≤ sin² θ − b θ′. Repeating the argument for the boundary points reached in the −g direction gives 0 ≤ 1 + b csc² θ θ′, equivalently 0 ≤ sin² θ + b θ′. Since sin θ > 0 in the parametrization, these two inequalities are equivalent to the product inequality sin⁴ θ − b² θ′² ≥ 0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Finite bending energy excludes curved boundary fans and gives C1 boundary regularity, Sect. 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E = 2μ ∫_S H² da be the bending energy, where μ > 0 and H is mean curvature. If a curved portion Tc of S is covered by rulings that all intersect at a boundary point, then its contribution to E is infinite unless H = 0 on Tc. Hence, if χ is C2 on the interior of D and E is finite, every such ruling fan is flat. Under the same finite-energy assumption, χ is C1 on all of ∂D.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a patch whose rulings meet at a point p, the paper uses polar-type parameters r̃(φ,ρ) = p + ρ g(φ). Its mean curvature is H(r̃(φ,ρ)) = n(φ) · g″(φ)/(2ρ). The area integral therefore contains ∫ (n(φ)·g″(φ))²/(4ρ) dρ dφ, which diverges at ρ = 0 unless n(φ)·g″(φ) vanishes, i.e. unless the patch is flat. For C1 boundary regularity, points with no ruling intersection already have a C2 extension by the earlier boundary-smoothness argument. At a ruling-intersection boundary point, finite energy makes the corresponding triangular reference region flat, so the deformation gradient Q = ∇χ is constant on that triangle. Since Q is uniform along each ruling and uniformly continuous on compact interior subsets hit by nearby rulings, the paper proves the differentiability estimate lim_{x→x°} |χ(x)−χ(x°)−Q°(x−x°)|/|x−x°| = 0. Thus ∇χ exists continuously at x°.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Dimensional reduction of bending energy and equivalence to the Wunderlich functional, Sects. 3.2-3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a closed ribbon S satisfying the ruled parametrization and non-overlap conditions, its bending energy E = 2μ ∫_S H² da can be written solely as an integral along the midline: E = (μ/2) ∫_{b}^{ℓ+b} |d″|²/(θ′ sin² θ) log((sin² θ + b θ′)/(sin² θ − b θ′)) dα, with the continuous limiting value |d″|²/(θ′ sin² θ) log((sin² θ + b θ′)/(sin² θ − b θ′)) → 2b |d″|²/sin⁴ θ as θ′→0. Equivalently, with V = sin² θ/θ′ and principal normal curvature κ̄_0 = |d″|/sin² θ, the energy is the Wunderlich functional E_W = (μ/2) ∫_{b}^{ℓ+b} κ̄_0² V log((V+b)/(V−b)) dα. The paper emphasizes that this derivation applies to orientable and nonorientable closed ribbons and to any number of twists, subject to the stated regularity and injectivity assumptions.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start from E = 2μ ∫_{0}^{ℓ+2b} ∫_{β_−}^{β_+} (H∘r̂)² |r̂_α × r̂_β| dβ dα. Apply the gluing/integral-reduction identity to replace the full directrix interval by α in [b,ℓ+b] and β in [−b csc θ,b csc θ]. Using r̂(α,β)=d(α)+βg(α), g′=−θ′(sin θ t−cos θ m), and the Darboux formulas, the paper computes 2(H∘r̂)²|r̂_α × r̂_β| = |d″|²/(2 sin² θ (sin θ − β θ′)). The non-overlap inequalities ensure the denominator and the logarithm argument are positive. The β-integral is elementary and gives the displayed one-dimensional formula. Substituting V = sin² θ/θ′ and Euler's curvature formula κ̄_0 = |d″|/sin² θ converts this expression exactly into Wunderlich's form.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Relaxed regularity and purely spatial isometry constraints, Sects. 4.1-4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper relaxes smoothness by assuming d is C1, d′ is piecewise C1, and g is continuous and piecewise C1 on [b,ℓ+b], with one-sided derivative limits at finitely many partition points. Under the ruled parametrization, and with c fixed as the relevant orientable or nonorientable reference directrix, the full isometry constraints |c′| = |d′| = 1, |f| = |g| = 1, |f′| = |g′|, c′ · f = d′ · g, and c′ · f′ = d′ · g′ are equivalent to the four spatial constraints |d′| = 1, |g| = 1, d″ · g = 0, and (d′ × g) · g′ = 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implication from the full constraints to the spatial ones is direct: |d′|=1 and |g|=1 are included; differentiating c′·f=d′·g and using c″=0 together with c′·f′=d′·g′ gives d″·g=0; and the Darboux-frame expression for g′ has no normal component, which gives (d′×g)·g′=0. Conversely, assume the four spatial constraints. Let θ be the angle between d′ and g and write g = cos θ t + sin θ m with t=d′. The endpoint formulas for the rulings require sin θ≠0. From d″·g=0 and t′=κ_g m+κ_n n, one gets κ_g sin θ=0, hence κ_g=0. The condition (d′×g)·g′=0 removes the normal component of g′, so g′ = −θ′(sin θ t−cos θ m). The reference formula f=cos θ c′+sin θ i3×c′ gives f′ = −θ′ sin θ c′ + θ′ cos θ i3×c′. Hence |f′|=|g′| and c′·f′=d′·g′, while c′·f=d′·g=cos θ. This recovers all full metric constraints.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Construction of a C1 ruled ribbon and locally injective isometric deformation from d and g, Sects. 4.3-4.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the closed-ribbon setting, suppose d and g on [b,ℓ+b] satisfy the relaxed regularity assumptions, the four spatial constraints |d′|=1, |g|=1, d″·g=0, (d′×g)·g′=0, and the local-injectivity inequality sin⁴ θ − b² θ′² ≥ 0, where θ is the angle between d′ and g. Define S = {d(α)+β g(α): α in [b,ℓ+b], β in [−b csc θ(α), b csc θ(α)]}. Then the ruled parametrization is locally injective in the interior, S is a C1 surface with piecewise continuous curvature, and, after using the appropriate orientable or nonorientable gluing extension across the short-edge seam, one can construct a locally injective isometric deformation χ from the rectangular strip D to S. The paper explicitly notes that this construction gives local injectivity, not necessarily global injectivity.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Local injectivity follows from the inverse function theorem once |(d′+βg′)×g| = |sin θ − β θ′| is nonzero for β in (−b csc θ,b csc θ). The two inequalities sin² θ ± b θ′ ≥ 0, equivalent to sin⁴ θ − b² θ′² ≥ 0, give this nonvanishing in the interior. The normal n = d′×g/|d′×g| is continuous by the regularity assumptions. To prove S is C1 at points where g′ jumps, the paper splits S into one-sided pieces S+ and S−, uses the common tangent plane A through the point, represents each side as a C1 graph over a half-disc in A separated by the ruling through the point, and observes that both graph gradients agree with the natural inclusion along the shared ruling; the two half-graphs therefore patch to a C1 parametrization. For the deformation, extend r̂ from [b,ℓ+b] to the full directrix interval [0,ℓ+2b] using the seam bijection α̂, define the reference f by f=cos θ c′+sin θ i3×c′, and set x̂=c+βf. The four spatial constraints imply the full metric constraints, so x̂ and r̂ induce an isometric deformation. The same inequality gives local injectivity for x̂, and since D and S are C1 the paper invokes Myers-Steenrod to conclude χ is C1. A separate global constraint would be required to ensure χ is an embedding.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Endpoint matching and linking-number constraints for twist, Sects. 5.1-5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For an orientable closed ribbon obtained by imposing the same-orientation edge condition after k full twists, the directrix and generatrix must satisfy d(b)=d(ℓ+b), d′(b)=d′(ℓ+b), and g(b)=g(ℓ+b). If M is the midline parametrized by d and M_ε is the perturbation d_ε(α)=d(α)+εg(α), with 0<ε<b csc θ° and θ°=min_{b≤α≤ℓ+b} θ(α), then the linking number satisfies Lk(M,M_ε)=∫_{b}^{ℓ+b}∫_{b}^{ℓ+b} ((d(α)−d_ε(ã))·(d′(α)×d′_ε(ã)))/(4π|d(α)−d_ε(ã)|³) dα dã = k. For a nonorientable closed ribbon obtained by a reversed edge condition after an odd nonzero number k of half-twists, the endpoint conditions are d(b)=d(ℓ+b), d′(b)=d′(ℓ+b), and g(b)=−g(ℓ+b). The perturbation is closed only after running twice around: d_ε(α)=d(α)+εg(α) for α in [b,ℓ+b) and d_ε(α)=d(α−ℓ)−εg(α−ℓ) for α in [ℓ+b,2ℓ+b], and the corresponding linking integral over α in [b,ℓ+b] and ã in [b,2ℓ+b] equals k. The value is independent of ε and the sign of k records the twisting direction.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The endpoint conditions are the direct translation of the two possible connecting edge conditions into the directrix/generatrix variables: the midline must close with matching tangent, and the ruling direction must match or reverse depending on orientability. To record the amount of twist, the paper uses the classical Gauss linking integral between the midline and a small offset curve lying on the ribbon. In the orientable case the offset by +εg closes after one circuit, so the usual integral over two copies of [b,ℓ+b] gives the number of full twists. In the nonorientable case +g changes sign after one circuit, so the offset curve closes only after a double traversal, with the second half using −g; the integral over the doubled offset curve gives the number of half-twists. Independence of the small ε is the standard homotopy invariance of linking number as long as the curves remain disjoint.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Constrained Euler-Lagrange system for equilibrium ribbons, Sect. 5.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Define an augmented one-dimensional energy for d and g by E*[d,g]=∫_{b}^{ℓ+b} (F(d′,g,g′)|d″|² − λ(|d′|²−1) − χ_m(|g|²−1) − γ d″·g − ν(d′×g)·g′ − ω G(d′,g,g′)) dα, where λ, χ_m, γ, and ν are scalar Lagrange multipliers, ω is a Karush-Kuhn-Tucker multiplier, s = sqrt(1−(d′·g)²), z = d′·g′, F = μ/(2 z s) log((s³+bz)/(s³−bz)) with continuous extension at z=0, and G = s⁴ − b² z²/s². If d is piecewise C3 and g is piecewise C2, stationarity under admissible variations yields the ODEs A′=0 and (|d″|² F_{g′})′ − |d″|² F_g + 2χ_m g − γ d″ − (ν d′×g)′ − ν d′×g′ + ω G_g − (ω G_{g′})′ = 0 on smooth subintervals, where A=(2F d″)′ − |d″|² F_{d′} + 2λd′ − (γg)′ + ν g×g′ + ω G_{d′} and subscripts denote Euclidean partial derivatives of F and G with respect to their vector arguments. The matching conditions at α=b and α=ℓ+b are A(ℓ+b)=A(b), B(ℓ+b)=B(b), and C(ℓ+b)=±C(b), where B=2F d″−γg, C=|d″|² F_{g′}−ν d′×g−ωG_{g′}, with plus sign for orientable ribbons and minus sign for nonorientable ribbons. At every interior point where curvature jumps, the jump conditions [A]=0, [B]=0, and [C]=0 hold. These are supplemented by the isometry constraints, the local-injectivity constraint G≥0, the KKT conditions ωG=0 and ω≥0, and the relevant endpoint and linking-number conditions.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper varies d and g in E*, treating F and G as functions of d′,g,g′. The first variation contains terms involving δd′′, δd′, δg′, and δg, plus the multiplier variations enforcing the constraints. Integrating by parts twice in the δd′′ terms and once in the δd′ and δg′ terms gives an integral over the smooth subintervals plus endpoint terms at b and ℓ+b and jump terms at the finitely many nonsmooth α_k. Since admissible variations are otherwise arbitrary, the coefficients of δd and δg in the integral give the displayed differential equations. The endpoint variations obey the orientable or nonorientable matching sign for g, producing the three matching conditions. The independent one-sided variations at curvature discontinuities force continuity of A, B, and C across each jump. The KKT equations are the standard stationarity/complementary-slackness conditions for the inequality G≥0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Nonuniqueness of rulings on flat portions, Sect. 5.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: On any maximal interval [α1,α2] along the midline for which d″=0, the Euler-Lagrange and jump conditions do not determine g uniquely. The paper shows that on such an interval the multiplier χ_m vanishes and one of the remaining equations expresses γ(α)g(α) in terms of an integral involving the arbitrary multiplier λ; since λ is arbitrary on the open interval, the ruling distribution g is not unique. This reflects the fact that the bending-energy density vanishes where d″=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If d″=0, then t=d′ is constant and all terms involving |d″|² drop out of the Euler-Lagrange equations. Resolving the second reduced equation in the basis {t,g,n} determines ν, χ_m, and ω, and its component along g gives χ_m=0. The first reduced equation can then be integrated from α1 to α, yielding the paper's formula for γ(α)g(α) in terms of ∫(2λt + νg×g′ + ωGd′) dα plus endpoint constants. The jump conditions constrain endpoint values but do not fix λ in the interior. Therefore different choices of λ lead to different admissible g on the flat portion without changing the energy.

proof_techniques.name: Encode ribbon topology by explicit edge identifications
proof_techniques.description: Rather than beginning with an abstract annulus or Möbius band, the paper keeps a rectangular reference strip D and imposes one of two short-edge matching conditions. Same-orientation matching gives an orientable closed ribbon, while reversed matching gives a nonorientable ribbon. This makes endpoint and seam behavior visible in coordinates and later converts topology into algebraic conditions on d and g.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The quotient of the infinite strip by the glide reflection G_beta is naturally represented by a finite rectangular fundamental domain with reversed end identification. This paper's coordinate-level treatment suggests proving facts on one fundamental domain and translating the G_beta-invariance into endpoint/seam constraints.

proof_techniques.name: Use a directrix that includes short-edge segments, not just the midline
proof_techniques.description: A central device is the reference directrix c(α), which follows part of a short edge, then the midline, then part of the other short edge. This choice guarantees that every ruling of the strip is hit exactly once and that the ruled parametrization covers the entire reference strip and deformed ribbon, including regions near the glued seam. The spatial directrix d=χ∘c therefore includes the midline plus the seam curve.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Clean triangulations have edges and vertices on the boundary, and seam-crossing behavior can be missed if one tracks only a centerline. A discrete analogue may require choosing a transversal or directrix that accounts for boundary/seam cells so that every triangle or ruling-like segment is counted once.

proof_techniques.name: Compare first fundamental forms of ruled parametrizations
proof_techniques.description: The paper represents the reference and spatial surfaces as x̂=c+βf and r̂=d+βg. Isometry becomes equality of the metric coefficients of these two parametrizations for every β, which reduces to a finite list of dot-product constraints: equality of directrix speeds, unit ruling directions, equality of |f′| and |g′|, and equality of the mixed dot products c′·f and c′·f′ with their spatial counterparts.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem uses piecewise-affine triangle maps with prescribed inequalities on edge lengths rather than exact isometry. A useful discrete analogue is to compare edge-length data triangle-by-triangle as metric coefficients with inequalities replacing equalities.

proof_techniques.name: Reduce developability and isometry to constraints on d and g alone
proof_techniques.description: After introducing the Darboux frame along the spatial directrix, the paper eliminates reference variables and shows that the full isometry constraints are equivalent to |d′|=1, |g|=1, d″·g=0, and (d′×g)·g′=0. These encode arclength parametrization, unit rulings, geodesic/directrix compatibility, and absence of a forbidden normal component in g′.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a PL clean triangulation, one can seek a purely spatial set of constraints or inequalities involving images of boundary vertices and cross-boundary edges, avoiding reference coordinates except for combinatorial incidence.

proof_techniques.name: No-overlap from monotonicity of boundary intercepts
proof_techniques.description: The local-injectivity inequality sin⁴ θ − b²θ′² ≥ 0 is obtained by comparing where neighboring rulings' boundary endpoints project back to geodesics normal to the midline. Nonintersection forces those projected intercepts to be monotone on both sides of the ribbon, giving the two inequalities sin² θ ± bθ′ ≥ 0.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original map is required to identify points only along G_beta-orbits, so any quotient proof must exclude unintended overlaps. A discrete version of the intercept-monotonicity argument could turn injectivity of a clean triangulated strip into inequalities on slopes or boundary vertex order.

proof_techniques.name: Change variables across the glued seam
proof_techniques.description: For rulings crossing the identified short edges, the paper constructs an explicit bijection α̂ between midline parameters and short-edge directrix parameters. Identities for r̂ and for the area Jacobian under this bijection transfer integrals over seam pieces to integrals over corresponding midline pieces. This is what lets the full surface integral reduce cleanly to one midline integral in both orientable and nonorientable cases.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A G_beta-invariant triangulation is infinite but periodic up to a flip. Seam change-of-variables suggests replacing global sums or counts by sums over one fundamental domain, with special care for triangles or edges crossing the reversed identification.

proof_techniques.name: Finite-energy exclusion of curved ruling fans
proof_techniques.description: The paper analyzes a family of rulings meeting at a boundary point and computes that the bending energy contains a divergent integral of the form ∫ dρ/ρ unless the patch is flat. This converts an analytic bounded-energy assumption into a geometric regularity conclusion: no curved boundary fans and C1 boundary behavior.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original problem is PL and not formulated with bending energy, an analogous compactness argument might penalize or rule out degenerate fans of clean triangles when passing to limiting triangulations or extremal configurations.

proof_techniques.name: Encode twist by a linking number of the midline and a small offset
proof_techniques.description: The number of full twists in the orientable case and half-twists in the nonorientable case is imposed by a Gauss linking integral between the midline and a small perturbation along the generatrix direction. In the nonorientable case the offset curve closes only after two traversals, so the perturbation is defined piecewise with g and then −g.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The glide reflection in the original problem fixes the half-twist topology of the quotient. A linking or parity invariant may help separate purely local squeeze inequalities from the global nonorientable obstruction responsible for the lower bound.

proof_techniques.name: Use KKT multipliers for local-injectivity inequalities
proof_techniques.description: The paper treats local injectivity as an inequality constraint G=sin⁴ θ−b²θ′² ≥ 0 and includes it in the variational functional with a Karush-Kuhn-Tucker multiplier. Complementary slackness then identifies where the no-overlap constraint is active.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one formulates the original infimum problem as a discrete constrained optimization over clean triangulations and vertex images, inequality constraints from squeeze and injectivity could be handled by an analogous active-constraint analysis.

other_useful_info: The paper repeatedly distinguishes local injectivity from global injectivity: its construction from d and g gives a locally injective isometric immersion, while global injectivity would require an additional global constraint that the authors leave to future work. It cites Halpern and Weaver (1977) for restrictions on the half-width-to-length ratio b/ℓ that guarantee existence of isometric immersions or embeddings of half-twist Möbius bands. It also cites Hartman and Nirenberg (1959) for the ruled structure of zero-Gaussian-curvature surfaces, Hartman and Wintner (1951) for regularity of asymptotic curves, Hornung (2011a,b,c) for fine structure and local injectivity of flat isometric immersions, and Chen, Fosdick, and Fried (2018) for the necessary-and-sufficient ruled-parametrization constraints. The authors note that the number of twists sustainable without loss of piecewise continuous curvature or without self-contact depends on the aspect ratio b/ℓ, but this paper does not give an explicit threshold. Sadowsky's construction is mentioned as evidence that relevant Möbius bands may be C1 and only piecewise C2, motivating relaxed regularity rather than assuming smoothness everywhere.
why_relevant: This gives a rigorous formulation of closed inextensible ribbons, including nonorientable cases, and carefully handles isometry and local injectivity constraints. It is useful for variational or compactness-based approaches to paper Möbius bands.

title: Möbius Bands, Unstretchable Material Sheets and Developable Surfaces
why_relevant: Chen and Fried clarify the distinction between being developable and being an isometric deformation of a prescribed flat strip. That distinction is important when trying to convert PL or smooth developable constructions into valid realizations of the given flat Möbius quotient.

title: Inverting a Cylinder Through Isometric Immersions and Isometric Embeddings
overall_summary: Halpern and Weaver study when a flat right circular cylinder can be turned inside out through isometric immersions or embeddings, and when a flat Möbius strip admits smooth isometric immersions or embeddings in R3. Their main cylinder result is that the inclusion of C_h = S1 × [-h,h] is isometrically homotopic to its inside-out version if and only if h < 1, with an explicit embedding isotopy for h < π/(π+2). Their main Möbius immersion result is sharp: M_l = ([0,l] × [0,1])/(0,y)~(l,1-y) isometrically immerses in R3 if and only if l > π/2. The paper also proves that embeddings are strictly harder than immersions, gives a non-sharp lower bound l > π/2 + ε for embedded Möbius strips, and gives the classical Sadowsky-type construction showing M_l isometrically embeds for l > √3. The core techniques are ruled-surface/generating-line structure of flat isometric immersions, a total-curvature estimate for antiparallel generating lines, a cohomological invariant built from set-valued families of generating directions, and a pseudofolding method that smooths explicit paper-folding patterns. For the original clean-triangulation problem, the most directly relevant content is the √3 Möbius embedding construction, the pseudofolding/fold-pattern method, and the total-curvature/generating-line obstruction strategy.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorems 2.1 and 2.2: mod-2 frame invariant for h < 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C_h = S1 × [-h,h] with its flat metric induced by the standard inclusion in R3, and let h < 1. For an isometric immersion f: C_h → R3, let N(p) be a unit normal to f at (p,0), and let T(p) be the unit tangent vector to the central curve f restricted to S1 × {0}. Define a loop γ: S1 → SO(3) by γ(p) = (T(p), N(p), T(p) × N(p)), viewed as an oriented orthonormal frame. The homotopy class G(f) = [γ] in π1(SO(3)) ≅ Z2 is invariant under isometric homotopy. If f has product form f = y × j: S1 × [-h,h] → R2 × R = R3, where y: S1 → R2 is a smooth closed unit-speed curve and j: [-h,h] → R is the inclusion, then G(f) is the tangent winding number of y reduced modulo 2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For h < 1 the invariant is defined directly from the frame along the central circle. An isometric homotopy f_t gives a continuous homotopy of the corresponding frame loops γ_t in SO(3), so the element of π1(SO(3)) is constant. In the product case y × j, the normal and tangent frame loop is exactly the standard frame determined by the tangent direction of the plane curve y together with the vertical direction; its class in π1(SO(3)) is the winding number of the tangent map S1 → S1, reduced mod 2 under the isomorphism π1(SO(3)) ≅ Z2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3: cohomology of allowable set-valued maps
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F: X → 2^Y be a set-valued map between Hausdorff spaces. It is called allowable if each value F(x) is nonempty and contractible, and the graph gr F = {(x,y) in X × Y : y in F(x)} is compact. For an allowable map F, define an induced homomorphism F*: H^q(Y) → H^q(X) in Alexander cohomology with integer coefficients as follows. Let P_X: gr F → X and P_Y: gr F → Y be the coordinate projections. Since every fiber P_X^{-1}(x) = {x} × F(x) is contractible, the Vietoris-Begle theorem implies P_X*: H^q(X) → H^q(gr F) is an isomorphism. Set F* = (P_X*)^{-1} ∘ P_Y*. This construction agrees with the ordinary induced map for a single-valued continuous map, is compatible with precomposition by a continuous single-valued map, and is invariant under allowable homotopy. Moreover, if F and G are allowable and F(x) ⊂ G(x) for all x, then F and G induce the same cohomology homomorphism because they are allowable-homotopic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key input is the Vietoris-Begle mapping theorem applied to P_X: gr F → X. The hypotheses hold because gr F is compact Hausdorff and the fibers x × F(x) have trivial reduced cohomology by contractibility. Thus P_X* is invertible and the displayed definition of F* makes sense. For a single-valued map f, gr F is homeomorphic to X and the formula reduces to f*. For precomposition g: Z → X, the graph of F ∘ g is compared with gr F by the map (z,y) ↦ (g(z),y), and the projection diagrams commute, giving (F ∘ g)* = g* ∘ F*. Homotopy invariance follows by applying the precomposition rule to the inclusions X × {0} and X × {1}, which are homotopic. If F(x) ⊂ G(x), the paper defines a piecewise constant allowable homotopy H(x,t) equal to F(x) for t < 1 and G(x) at t = 1, whose graph is compact because it is the union of gr F × I and gr G × {1}; hence F* = G*.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4: upper semicontinuity of allowable maps
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If F: X → 2^Y is allowable, x is a point of X, and V is an open subset of Y containing F(x), then there exists a neighborhood U of x in X such that F(x') ⊂ V for every x' in U.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the compact-graph upper semicontinuity property. If no such U existed, one could find a net or sequence x_i → x and points y_i in F(x_i) outside V. Compactness of gr F gives a convergent subnet (x_i,y_i) → (x,y) in gr F. Then y lies in F(x) ⊂ V, contradicting that the complement of V contains all y_i and is closed after passing to a suitable subnet outside V.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.1: structure of generating line segments for flat cylinder immersions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f: C_h → R3 be an isometric immersion, where C_h = S1 × [-h,h]. Let π: R × [-h,h] → C_h be the universal covering map and set g = f ∘ π. For s in R define V_g(s) to be the set of all real t such that the endpoints (s-t,-h) and (s+t,h) are mapped by g at their intrinsic Euclidean distance, i.e. ||g(s+t,h) - g(s-t,-h)|| = ||(s+t,h) - (s-t,-h)||. Equivalently, the straight segment joining those two points in the covering strip is mapped rigidly. For p in S1, define V(p) = V_g(s), where π(s,0) = (p,0). Then: (a) for each p, V(p) is a nonempty closed line segment; (b) the graph of V and the image of V are compact; (c) there exist monotone nondecreasing functions T,B: R → R satisfying T(s+2π) = T(s)+2π, B(s+2π) = B(s)+2π, (T(s)+B(s))/2 = s, and T(s)-s in V_g(s) for all s. In addition, if a, β are real numbers and t in V_g(a), t' in V_g(β) are chosen so that the corresponding two cross-strip line segments do not cross in the covering strip, then T and B may be chosen with T(a)-a = t and T(β)-β = t'.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses standard facts about smooth isometric immersions of flat surfaces: through each interior point of nonzero mean curvature there is a unique straight line segment lying on the surface; when extended, it remains on the surface until it hits the boundary, and nonzero mean curvature persists along it. Applied to g on the covering strip, this line segment through (s,0) cannot be horizontal, because a horizontal ruling would be periodic and contradict immersion/covering behavior. Hence, at every central point with nonzero mean curvature, there is a unique segment from bottom boundary to top boundary, giving a singleton V_g(s). At boundary points of the nonzero-mean-curvature set, take monotone limits of nearby top and bottom endpoints; monotonicity is forced because crossing rulings would contradict uniqueness of rulings on the flat surface. On open intervals where the mean curvature along the central curve vanishes, the central curve maps as a straight segment; using the already-defined endpoint rulings at the ends of such an interval, elementary Euclidean geometry shows that the whole quadrilateral bounded by the two endpoint rulings and top and bottom boundary intervals is mapped rigidly. In such a quadrilateral, V_g(s) is a closed interval and T,B can be defined by linear interpolation. Periodicity comes from the covering translation. Compactness of the graph follows by first observing that all t in all V(p) are uniformly bounded: otherwise endpoints in the compact cylinder would map too far apart. Closedness follows directly from the defining equality, and compactness of the image is its projection.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.2: compactness of generating-line data in homotopies
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H: C_h × I → R3 be a homotopy through isometric immersions. For each time τ in I, define V_{H_τ} as in Proposition 4.1, and define V_H(p,τ) = V_{H_τ}(p). Then the graph of V_H and the image of V_H are compact.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is the same compactness argument as in Proposition 4.1, with the homotopy parameter included. The cylinder C_h and the interval of parameters are compact, so the image of H is uniformly bounded. This gives a uniform bound on all possible t-values in V_H. Closedness of the graph follows from continuity of H and the distance equality defining V, and hence the graph is compact.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.1: antiparallel generating lines are separated by more than πh
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f: C_h → R3 be an isometric immersion, let g: R × [-h,h] → R3 be its lift, and let V_g(s) be as in Proposition 4.1. Suppose a < β, t in V_g(a), and t' in V_g(β). If the two generating vectors are antiparallel in the sense that g(a+t,h) - g(a-t,-h) = λ(g(β+t',h) - g(β-t',-h)) for some λ < 0, then β - a > πh.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Using Proposition 4.1, choose monotone functions T,B with T(a)-a=t and T(β)-β=t'. Define the top and bottom curves in R3 by Tbar(s)=g(T(s),h) and Bbar(s)=g(B(s),-h), and define φ(s)=Tbar(s)-Bbar(s). For every s, the segment from (B(s),-h) to (T(s),h) is mapped rigidly, so ||φ(s)|| = sqrt((T(s)-B(s))^2 + (2h)^2) ≥ 2h. The antiparallel hypothesis says φ(a) and φ(β) lie on opposite rays through the origin. Any curve in R3 staying outside the open ball of radius 2h and joining a ray to the opposite ray has length at least the semicircle length 2πh. Thus length(φ|[a,β]) ≥ 2πh. On the other hand, length(φ) ≤ length(Tbar) + length(Bbar), and because g is isometric and T,B are monotone, these lengths are T(β)-T(a) and B(β)-B(a). With T(a)=a+t, T(β)=β+t', B(a)=a-t, and B(β)=β-t', their sum is 2(β-a). Hence β-a ≥ πh. The paper then rules out equality: equality would force ||φ(s)||=2h throughout, hence T(s)=B(s)=s, and force equality in the triangle inequality, so the top and bottom derivatives are oppositely directed. This makes the derivative of the central curve vanish, contradicting that g is an isometric immersion. Therefore β-a > πh.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.2: lower bound for isometric immersion of a flat Möbius strip
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M_l = ([0,l] × [0,1])/(0,y)~(l,1-y) be the flat Möbius strip of length l and width 1. If M_l admits a smooth isometric immersion into R3, then l > π/2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper notes that this follows either directly from the proof of Proposition 5.1 or by passing to the double cover of the Möbius strip by a flat cylinder. In the double cover, the Möbius identification reverses the transverse direction, so a generating line and its translate by one Möbius length become antiparallel. Applying Proposition 5.1 with half-width h = 1/2 forces the horizontal separation l to be greater than πh = π/2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.1: deformation retract of the space Q
