key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let e3 = (0,0,1) in R3, let R = {t e3 : t < 0} be the downward open ray, and define Q = {(u,v) in (R3 \ R) × R3 : u and v are linearly independent}. Embed S1 in Q by j(p) = (e3,p), viewing S1 as the unit circle in the xy-plane. Then j(S1) is a strong deformation retract of Q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First apply Gram-Schmidt to deform any pair (u,v) in Q to (u/||u||, v_perp/||v_perp||), where the second vector is the normalized component of v perpendicular to u. Then rotate the first vector u/||u|| to e3 along the shorter geodesic arc on S2; the excluded downward ray ensures this can be done continuously without ambiguity. Apply the same rotation to the second vector. The result is a pair (e3,p) with p in the xy-plane unit circle. Points already of the form (e3,p) remain fixed, giving a strong deformation retract.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Sections 7-8: integer invariant G(f) for h > 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let h > 1 and let f: C_h → R3 be an isometric immersion. With g, V_g, and the generating vectors as above, define W(p) to be the set of image vectors from the center point f(p,0) to the top endpoint of a generating segment through (p,0). Choose w in W(0) and a rotation U in SO(3) taking w to a positive multiple of e3. Let T(p) be the unit tangent to the central curve. Define a set-valued map φ: S1 → 2^Q by φ(p) = U(W(p),T(p)), where Q is the space in Lemma 6.1. Then φ is allowable. Let η be a generator of H^1(S1;Z), and define G(f) in Z by φ* j*^{-1}(η) = G(f)η, where j: S1 → Q is the deformation-retract inclusion. The integer G(f) is independent of the choices of w and U. If f = y × j0: S1 × [-h,h] → R2 × R = R3 is a product isometric immersion with y a smooth closed unit-speed plane curve and j0 the interval inclusion, then G(f) is the tangent winding number of y.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key point is that, when h > 1, Proposition 5.1 prevents any generating vector from becoming antiparallel to the chosen vector w within one period: antiparallel generating lines would have to be separated by more than πh > π, but on a circle one can choose representatives separated by at most π. Thus, after the rotation U, the first component avoids the forbidden downward ray R, so φ takes values in Q. Proposition 4.1 gives that each W(p) is a nonempty closed line segment and that the graph is compact; hence φ is allowable. Lemma 6.1 identifies H^1(Q) with Z through j, allowing the definition of G(f). Independence of U follows because the rotations carrying w to the positive e3-axis form a path-connected circle, and varying U along a path gives an allowable homotopy of set-valued maps. Independence of w follows because W(0) is a line segment; moving w linearly to another choice w' and choosing compatible rotations again gives an allowable homotopy. In the product case, the generating set is vertical and the resulting map to Q retracts to the tangent map of the plane curve y, so the integer is exactly the tangent winding number.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.1: local constancy of G(f)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let h > 1 and let f: C_h → R3 be an isometric immersion. Define a closeness measure between isometric immersions f and f' by p(f,f') = sup_{x in C_h} ||f(x)-f'(x)|| + sup_{s in R} ||∂_s(g(s,0)-g'(s,0))||, where g and g' are the lifted maps to R × [-h,h]. Then there exists δ > 0 such that every isometric immersion f' with p(f,f') < δ satisfies G(f') = G(f).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a careful stability argument for the allowable set-valued map φ. First, Lemma 8.2 is an elementary compactness lemma controlling zero sets of nonnegative functions under small perturbations. Lemma 8.3 applies it to the defining distance defect for V_g(s), showing that if the graph of φ_f lies in an open set M, then for f' sufficiently close the graph of φ_{f'} also lies in M. Lemma 8.4 similarly shows W_{f'}(0) lies in a small neighborhood of W_f(0). Lemma 8.5 gives a uniform ε-neighborhood of the image of φ_f that remains inside Q after any rotation usable in the definition of G(f). Next, using upper semicontinuity of allowable maps, the graph of φ_f is enclosed in the interior of a piecewise constant allowable map ψ: S1 → 2^{R3×R3} with convex values and image contained in a small neighborhood of φ_f. For f' close, after choosing a rotation U' compatible with a vector w' in W_{f'}(0), one has φ_{f'}(p) ⊂ ψ(p), while φ_f(p) ⊂ ψ(p) as well. Since inclusion of allowable maps induces homotopy, φ_f, ψ, and φ_{f'} have the same induced cohomology map. Therefore G(f') = G(f).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 8.6 and Corollary 8.7: winding obstruction to cylinder inversion for h > 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let y × j and y' × j be product isometric immersions S1 × [-h,h] → R2 × R = R3, where y,y' are smooth closed unit-speed plane curves. If these product immersions are isometrically homotopic and h > 1, then the tangent winding numbers of y and y' are equal. If h < 1, then their tangent winding numbers are equal modulo 2. Consequently, if h > 1, the standard inclusion i: C_h → R3 is not isometrically homotopic to the inside-out map r(x,y,z) = (x,y,-z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For h > 1, use the integer invariant G(f) of Sections 7-8, its homotopy invariance from Theorem 8.1, and the product formula G(y×j)=winding(y). For h < 1, use the mod-2 frame invariant of Section 2. For the corollary, rotate the inside-out map r through an ambient rotation to a product map whose plane curve is the reflection of the unit circle, so its tangent winding number is -1, while the inclusion has winding number +1. Since +1 and -1 are distinct integers, h > 1 forbids an isometric homotopy.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 9.1: unit of pseudofolding
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a strip of half-width h. For each positive strip length l, there is a smooth isotopy ψ: [0,l] × [-h,h] × [0,θ(l)] → R3 through isometric embeddings, written ψ_t(x,y)=ψ(x,y,t), with the following properties. Initially ψ_0(x,y)=(x,y,0). A front margin x ≤ ε is fixed throughout. A back margin x ≥ l-ε undergoes, up to a time-dependent translation T_t, a rotation R_t about the original x-axis by angle t radians: ψ_t(x,y)=T_t ∘ R_t(x,y) there. The middle portion stays horizontally between the two margins. For all points and times, if ψ_t(x,y)=(a,b,c), then sqrt(b^2+c^2) ≤ h + l e(l), where e(l) → 0 as l → 0. Finally, the total twist angle satisfies θ(l) = (1 + A(l)) l/h with A(l) → 0 as l → 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The authors first construct a pseudofold: choose a smooth family of unit-speed plane curves γ_t that agree with a straight line outside a small interval and whose curvature is a scaled bump with total turning t. Crossing γ_t with a line gives a smooth isometric bending of a planar strip that approximates an actual fold along a crease line; the small curved neighborhood is the fudge factor. A unit of pseudofolding is built from three such pseudofolds along three pseudocrease lines arranged as in Figure 9.3. The first and third pseudofolds are assigned equal angles t. The middle pseudofold angle θ_2(t) is chosen smoothly so that the image of a central rigid segment in the left construction matches the corresponding segment in the right construction after a rotation about the x-axis and a translation. This matching is verified by comparing the projected directions of the segment and the surface normals. Reparameterizing the homotopy by the resulting rotation angle gives the stated back-margin twist. As the fudge factor tends to zero, the construction converges to the ideal folded pattern in Figure 9.1, which gives the estimates: the motion stays inside a cylinder of radius h + o(l), and the twist achieved per unit length is asymptotically 1/h.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorems 10.2 and 10.3: cylinder inversion by pseudofolding
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let i: C_h → R3 be the standard inclusion and r: C_h → R3 be the inside-out map r(x,y,z)=(x,y,-z). Then i is isometrically homotopic to r if and only if h < 1. Moreover, if h < π/(π+2), then i is isometrically isotopic to r through isometric embeddings.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The necessity for homotopy when h > 1 is Corollary 8.7. For the constructive direction, the paper describes an explicit inversion using the pseudofolding unit of Lemma 9.1. The cylinder is viewed as a flexible tape. Starting from the standard cylinder, an accordion-like folded region is created. Two pseudofolding machines feed tape into banks of completed pseudofolding units while an accordion rotates through 180 degrees. After the midway position, the process is reversed on the other side to obtain the inside-out cylinder. In an immersion homotopy, the two banks of completed units may pass through each other, so the only essential length cost is two banks each requiring asymptotic length πh; the circumference 2π must exceed 2πh, giving h < 1. For an embedding isotopy, extra length is needed to keep two accordions and two banks disjoint. The paper gives the length requirement 2π > 2h + 2h + πh + πh, equivalent to h < π/(π+2). The pseudofolding estimates ensure that, by using sufficiently small units, the ideal folded motion can be approximated by smooth isometric embeddings or immersions with the same length inequalities.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 11.1: classification of product isometric immersions up to isometric homotopy
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let y × j and y' × j be product isometric immersions S1 × [-h,h] → R2 × R = R3, where y,y': S1 → R2 are smooth closed unit-speed plane curves and j is the inclusion of [-h,h] in R. If h > 1, then y × j and y' × j are isometrically homotopic if and only if y and y' have the same tangent winding number. If h < 1, then they are isometrically homotopic if and only if their tangent winding numbers are equal modulo 2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The only-if direction is Proposition 8.6. For the if direction with h > 1, use the Whitney-Graustein theorem: two regular closed plane curves are regularly homotopic if and only if they have the same tangent winding number. Given a regular homotopy between y and y', rescale each intermediate curve and reparameterize by arclength to obtain an isometric homotopy of unit-speed curves; crossing with the interval inclusion gives the desired isometric homotopy of cylinders. For h < 1, it remains to show that the winding number can be changed by ±2 through an isometric homotopy. The authors first homotope y to a curve y' with a long circular arc of length exceeding 2πh and a small leftover segment. On the corresponding cylindrical part, they apply the cylinder inversion construction of Sections 9-10 while holding the leftover segment fixed. This reflects the circular arc and changes the tangent winding by 2 or -2. Repeating this move reduces the problem to the equal-winding case.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 12.1: sharp smooth isometric immersion criterion for flat Möbius strips
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M_l = ([0,l] × [0,1])/(0,y)~(l,1-y) be the flat Möbius strip of length l and width 1. Then M_l admits a smooth isometric immersion into R3 if and only if l > π/2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The lower bound l > π/2 is Corollary 5.2. For the upper construction, the paper divides a strip of paper into three rectangular sections. In the first rectangle ABCD, draw an odd number n of pseudocrease lines, each making a small angle w with the vertical, chosen so that n·2w = π. Pseudofolding alternately back and forth along these lines brings the directed edge CD into a position antiparallel to AB. As the gaps between successive pseudocreases and the fudge factors tend to zero, the folded rectangle approaches an ideal folded rectangle in which CD lands on AB, so D can be made arbitrarily close to A. The second rectangle CDFE has two parallel pseudocrease lines; by choosing the folding angle, EF is brought to the position of BA translated perpendicularly. The third rectangle EFHG is then used to join GH smoothly to BA, completing the Möbius identification. By taking n large and the gaps and fudge factors small, the required length of the first rectangle approaches π/2 while the extra lengths in the second and third rectangles can be made arbitrarily small. Thus every l > π/2 is achieved.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 13: near-critical inversions must have many near-folds
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S: C_h × I → R3 be an isometric homotopy from the inclusion i to the inside-out map r, with h close to 1. At some intermediate time τ, the immersion f = S_τ has a pair of antiparallel generating lines. Choosing lifted parameters a < β with β-a ≤ π and using the notation of Proposition 5.1, one obtains β-a > πh and hence β-a is close to π when h is close to 1. For the associated top and bottom endpoint functions T,B and vector φ(s)=g(T(s),h)-g(B(s),-h), the paper derives the following near-equality consequences as h → 1: ||φ(s)|| is uniformly close to 2h; φ(s) stays close to a fixed plane P through φ(a), φ((a+β)/2), and φ(β); outside a set of arbitrarily small measure, φ'(s) is nearly perpendicular to φ(s) and nearly lies in P on a fixed side. Also T(s)-B(s) is uniformly small, T'(s)+B'(s)=2, T',B' ≥ 0, ∫_a^β |T'(s)-B'(s)| ds ≥ 2πh, and |T'(s)-B'(s)| is near 2 for most s. Consequently, along any subinterval of nontrivial size, T'-B' spends roughly half the time near +2 and half near -2. One can choose an arbitrarily long sequence a < s1 < ... < sN < β such that the surface normals near (s_i,0) alternate sign; the central curve is nearly stationary, has high curvature distributed densely, and the boundary curves nearly lie on a circle of radius h.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The starting point is the invariant obstruction: during an inversion, the generating-line data must pass through a configuration with antiparallel generating lines. Proposition 5.1 gives β-a > πh, while periodicity gives β-a ≤ π, so all inequalities used in Proposition 5.1 are nearly equalities for h close to 1. Nearly minimal length of φ outside the ball of radius 2h forces φ to run close to a semicircle in a plane and forces ||φ|| close to 2h. Since the surface is flat and the normal is constant along a straight ruling, the derivative ∂g/∂s is constant along each generating line. This gives the identity φ'(s)=∂_s g(s,0)(T'(s)-B'(s)). The relations (T+B)/2=s, T',B'≥0 imply T'+B'=2 and |T'-B'|≤2. The total-curvature lower bound gives ∫|T'-B'|≥2πh, so for h close to 1 the absolute value is almost always maximal. But T-B remains small, so the signed integral of T'-B' over subintervals is small, forcing oscillation between +2 and -2. These alternating regimes imply alternating tangent directions and surface normals, which is interpreted as many near-folds. The paper states that the simplifying assumption that T and B are continuously differentiable can be removed as in Proposition 5.1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 14.1: bounded mean curvature prevents near-critical inversion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each B > 0, the supremum of h such that C_h can be inverted through isometric immersions whose mean curvatures are bounded in absolute value by B is strictly less than 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Section 13 shows that any inversion with h sufficiently close to 1 must pass through a stage where the central curve is almost stationary but has very many near-folds, with tangent direction changing by nearly π over arbitrarily small parameter intervals. This forces arbitrarily large curvature of the central curve. For a flat isometric immersion of the cylinder, this central-curve curvature is equivalent to a lower bound on the surface mean curvature at corresponding points. Therefore a uniform mean-curvature bound B rules out inversions for all h sufficiently close to 1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 15.1: embedded Möbius strips require length strictly larger than π/2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There exists ε > 0 such that if the flat Möbius strip M_l = ([0,l] × [0,1])/(0,y)~(l,1-y) admits a smooth isometric embedding into R3, then l > π/2 + ε.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The near-critical analysis of Section 13 applies to an immersion of M_l when l is close to π/2. Choose coordinates so that the plane P from that analysis is the xy-plane. For l sufficiently close to π/2, the generating line segments are nearly horizontal, their centers are all nearly at one point, and the generating vector φ(t+1/2) is nearly at right angles to φ(t). Hence the projections to the xy-plane of two generating segments E = [B(t),T(t)] and F = [B(t+1/2),T(t+1/2)] intersect in a unique point p. Let q in E and r in F be the points lying over p in R3, and define v(t) as the vertical distance from q to r. If the immersion is an embedding, then q and r are never the same point, so v(t) ≠ 0 for all t. The function v(t) is continuous on [0,1/2], so its sign is constant. But by the Möbius identification and the choice of paired segments, v(0) = -v(1/2), contradiction. Thus no embedding exists for l sufficiently close to π/2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 15: Sadowsky-type upper construction for embedded Möbius strips
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper states that a paper Möbius strip can be made by folding the strip shown in Figure 15.1 along the indicated fold lines, and that using this fold pattern together with the pseudofold technique implies that M_l isometrically embeds in R3 whenever l > √3. The paper notes that M. Sadowsky gave a similar embedding.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The text gives the construction by reference to Figure 15.1 rather than by a full coordinate proof. The intended argument is the same smoothing principle used earlier: start with an explicit embedded polyhedral paper model made by folding a flat strip along the indicated creases; the model has critical length √3. Replace the sharp folds by pseudofolds as in Lemma 9.1, with sufficiently small fudge factors, to obtain smooth isometric embeddings. The smoothing requires an arbitrarily small extra amount of length, hence every l > √3 is realized. The paper presents this as an easy consequence of the displayed fold pattern and the pseudofolding machinery.

proof_techniques.name: Generating-line analysis for flat isometric immersions
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the fact that a smooth flat surface in R3 is ruled away from zero-mean-curvature planar regions. For a lifted cylinder g: R × [-h,h] → R3, this yields line segments running from one boundary component to the other that are mapped rigidly. The set of all such segments through a central point is encoded by V(s), and Proposition 4.1 proves that V(s) is always a compact interval and can be parameterized by monotone top and bottom endpoint functions T(s), B(s).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem uses affine maps on clean triangles with vertices on the boundary of a strip. A discrete analogue of generating lines may be the non-boundary edges of the clean triangles, especially because squeeze maps lengthen the two non-boundary edges and shorten the boundary edge.

proof_techniques.name: Total-curvature estimate from antiparallel rulings
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 5.1 converts the existence of two antiparallel generating vectors into a quantitative separation bound. The vector φ(s) joining bottom to top endpoints stays outside a ball of radius 2h; if its endpoints lie on opposite rays, it must have length at least the semicircle length 2πh. But the length of φ is bounded above by the sum of lengths of the top and bottom endpoint curves, equal to 2(β-a). This gives β-a > πh.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A lower-bound proof for realized β may try to force an antiparallel or crossing configuration in the quotient Möbius strip and then bound horizontal separation by a total-curvature or turning argument. The clean-triangle setting may require a polygonal version of the same estimate, replacing curve length by sums over edges.

proof_techniques.name: Set-valued cohomological invariant
proof_techniques.description: For h > 1, the family of generating directions need not be single-valued, so the paper builds a cohomology class from an allowable set-valued map φ: S1 → 2^Q. The values are contractible line segments, the graph is compact, and the Vietoris-Begle theorem gives a well-defined pullback in cohomology. After normalizing one generating vector, Proposition 5.1 ensures that all values avoid a forbidden ray, so the map lands in a space Q deformation-retracting to S1. The resulting integer G(f) is locally constant and hence homotopy invariant.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the clean triangulation gives multiple possible diagonals or rulings through a point, a set-valued invariant may avoid choosing a ruling continuously. This is useful when combinatorics change along the strip or under limiting arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Pseudofolding: smoothing an origami fold pattern
proof_techniques.description: A pseudofold replaces an ideal crease by a narrow smooth region whose curvature is a bump function with prescribed total turning. It is constructed from a plane curve with compactly supported curvature and crossed with a line. Several pseudofolds can be composed along prescribed pseudocrease lines to approximate a folded paper model while remaining a smooth isometric embedding. The estimates in Lemma 9.1 quantify how much twist is produced per unit length and how close the motion stays to the ideal folded model.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is already piecewise affine, so one may not need to smooth folds; nevertheless, the fold-pattern part of pseudofolding is directly relevant for constructing maps on clean triangulations. The technique explains how a local triangular or crease pattern can accumulate a desired half-twist with controlled length.

proof_techniques.name: Length accounting by accumulated twist
proof_techniques.description: In the cylinder inversion construction, each small pseudofolding unit of length l produces twist asymptotic to l/h. A 180-degree rotation therefore costs about πh length. The global construction becomes a bookkeeping problem: count how many banks of pseudofolding units and accordions are needed, and compare their total length to the circumference 2π.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the quotient strip in the original problem, β is the available length around the Möbius direction. A proof of the upper bound can similarly be organized by giving a repeatable local unit and summing its required horizontal length to reach a half-twist.

proof_techniques.name: Sharp immersion lower bound via double cover of the Möbius strip
proof_techniques.description: The Möbius immersion lower bound l > π/2 is obtained by passing to the double-covering cylinder and observing that the Möbius flip turns a generating line into an antiparallel one after one strip length. Proposition 5.1 then applies with half-width h = 1/2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original quotient by (x,y) ↦ (x+β,1-y) is exactly a flat Möbius band. Many arguments may become simpler on the double cover, where the flip becomes a relation between data separated by β.

proof_techniques.name: Near-equality analysis of the total-curvature bound
proof_techniques.description: When h is close to the critical value 1, Proposition 5.1 is nearly sharp. The paper exploits equality cases in every step of the proof: φ must nearly trace a semicircle of radius 2h, T-B must be small, and T'-B' must alternate between nearly +2 and nearly -2. This forces many near-folds and large curvature.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For an optimal √3 lower bound, one may need to analyze near-extremal configurations rather than just prove a coarse obstruction. The paper shows how to mine equality conditions in a length/turning inequality for structural information.

proof_techniques.name: Embedding obstruction by vertical crossing sign
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 15.1 distinguishes embeddings from immersions near the π/2 threshold. Near criticality, two projected generating segments must cross in the xy-plane. For an embedding, the vertical separation at their crossing cannot vanish and hence has a locally constant sign. The Möbius symmetry forces the sign to reverse over half a period, yielding a contradiction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original realized condition includes injectivity modulo the deck transformation. A discrete version of this sign-change obstruction could rule out short embedded or quotient-injective configurations by forcing projected crossings with inconsistent over-under data.

proof_techniques.name: Sadowsky fold pattern plus smoothing
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses a displayed fold pattern, attributed as similar to Sadowsky's, to produce embedded Möbius strips for every l > √3. The construction is polyhedral at the ideal level and becomes smooth by replacing sharp folds with pseudofolds. The strict inequality l > √3 appears because smoothing the ideal creases requires arbitrarily small extra length.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the paper's most direct source for the √3 upper construction. In the clean-triangulation setting, the ideal fold pattern may be usable without smoothing, provided its triangular faces and edge-length inequalities can be matched to the required squeeze maps.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly conjectures that M_l isometrically embeds in R3 only if l > √3; this is the natural counterpart to its Sadowsky-type upper construction and was not proved in the paper. It also conjectures that there is ε > 0 such that the cylinder inclusion is isometrically isotopic to its inside-out version only if h < 1-ε. The cited related works most relevant to these methods are Sadowsky [7] and Wunderlich [11] on developable Möbius bands, Hartman-Nirenberg [5] and Chern-Lashof [2] on flat immersed surfaces and total curvature, Whitney [10] on regular homotopy of plane curves, and Barr [1, Chapter 3], which the paper says gives isometric but nonsmooth embeddings of arbitrarily short Möbius strips.
why_relevant: This is the original Halpern-Weaver paper containing the classical pi/2 lower bound, the sqrt(3) upper construction, and the conjecture solved later by Schwartz. Its homotopy and total-curvature methods are foundational for any proof of an optimal aspect-ratio statement.

title: The Optimal Paper Moebius Band and Finite Tensegrity Calculations
overall_summary: This research note studies the conjecture that an embedded paper Möbius band has aspect ratio at least √3, with near-minimizers converging to an equilateral triangle of perimeter 2√3. The main strategy is to cut a polygonal paper Möbius band along a special pair of perpendicular coplanar bend images, normalize the resulting two trapezoids, and convert both topological embeddedness constraints and metric constraints into finitely many tensegrity capacity inequalities. The note proves several structural reductions and a priori estimates, but it explicitly presents the work as research notes and does not supply a completed rigorous verification of all finite inequalities needed for the final conjectures. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the finite edge-inequality viewpoint: clean triangulations with affine maps can be encoded by selected bend/ridge vertices, capacities, crossing diagrams, and forced incidences whose lower bounds are optimized by an equilateral triangle.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Figure 1.1 / standard upper-bound construction
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper recalls the standard construction giving the upper bound for the optimal paper Möbius problem: there is an immersed polygonal paper Möbius band of aspect ratio exactly √3 whose limiting image is an equilateral triangle of semiperimeter √3, equivalently perimeter 2√3. This limiting polygonal image is not embedded. However, for every ε>0, this limiting map can be approximated by smooth embedded paper Möbius bands of aspect ratio √3+ε; the paper refers to Fuchs-Tabachnikov for discussion of this approximation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The note gives the construction pictorially rather than as a full proof. One folds a flat Möbius band by polygonal facets so that, after a rotation/gluing operation, the image collapses onto the three sides of an equilateral triangle of perimeter 2√3. The exact limiting configuration has self-contact or non-embeddedness, so it is not itself an embedded paper Möbius band. By perturbing the folds and allowing an arbitrarily small increase in aspect ratio, one separates the self-contact and smooths the polygonal model, obtaining smooth embeddings of aspect ratio √3+ε. The details are cited rather than developed in this note.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1 (existence of a T-pattern, from [S2])
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M=(λ,I) be a polygonal paper Möbius band, meaning that I is an isometric embedding of Mλ=([0,1]×[0,λ])/(x,0)∼(1−x,λ) into R3 and is affine on each triangle of a triangulation whose vertices lie on the boundary of Mλ. For a triangle, its ridge is the edge contained in the boundary, its apex is the opposite vertex, a bend is a segment joining the apex to a ridge point, and a bend image is the image of such a bend under I. A T-pattern is a pair of disjoint bend images that are perpendicular and coplanar. If λ<7π/12, then the polygonal paper Möbius band has a T-pattern.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof considers the space P of ordered pairs of perpendicular bend images. For small aspect ratio, the band can be rotated so that all bend images make angle less than π/4 with the XY-plane; consequently the parallel planes containing a perpendicular pair do not contain vertical line segments. Generically P is a one-dimensional manifold. The argument identifies a connected component K of P that is invariant under the involution swapping the two bend images. Starting with a pair (α,β) in K and moving along a path in K to (β,α), the corresponding pair of parallel planes exchanges order. Since the planes never become vertical, their signed separation changes continuously from one sign to the other. At an intermediate time the planes coincide, so the two perpendicular bend images are coplanar. This gives the T-pattern.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.4 (conditional proof of Optimality and Rigidity from Lemmas 2.2 and 2.3)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume the Topology Lemma 2.2 and the Geometry Lemma 2.3, including the versions obtained by interchanging the two normalized trapezoids τ1 and τ2. Then no embedded polygonal paper Möbius band has aspect ratio less than √3. Moreover, any sequence of embedded polygonal paper Möbius bands with aspect ratios tending to √3 has images which, after Euclidean isometries, converge in the Hausdorff metric to an equilateral triangle of perimeter 2√3. Together with polygonal approximation of smooth paper Möbius bands, this yields the corresponding conditional optimality and rigidity statements for smooth embedded paper Möbius bands.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Suppose first that an embedded polygonal paper Möbius band has aspect ratio λ<√3. Since √3<7π/12, Lemma 2.1 gives a T-pattern. Cut open along one bend of the T-pattern and use the paper's standard normalization, producing two trapezoids τ1 and τ2 with boundary-length sums S1=L1+R1 and S2=L2+R2 and with λ=(S1+S2)/2. Thus S1+S2<2√3. Relabel so S2<√3. By the symmetric form of Geometry Lemma 2.3, the incidence τ2→1τ2 is impossible, because it would imply S2≥√3. Apply Topology Lemma 2.2 to the swapped pair (τ2,τ1). The alternatives τ2→4τ1 and τ1→4τ2 are ruled out by Geometry Lemma 2.3 and its symmetric version, since each would imply S1+S2≥2√3. Hence the only remaining alternative is τ1→1τ2. Now apply Topology Lemma 2.2 to the original ordered pair (τ1,τ2). Its four alternatives are τ1→1τ1, τ2→1τ1, τ1→4τ2, and τ2→4τ1. The last two are again ruled out by the second case of the Geometry Lemma and its symmetric version. The first, combined with τ1→1τ2, is ruled out by case 4 of the Geometry Lemma, while the second, combined with τ1→1τ2, is ruled out by case 3. This contradiction proves the conditional lower bound. For rigidity, take a sequence with λn→√3 and choose T-patterns and tame pairs. The Topology Lemma supplies one of the incidence alternatives, and the Geometry Lemma's moreover clause forces the convex hull of the T-pattern to be arbitrarily close to an equilateral triangle of perimeter 2√3 whenever equality is approached. Since the boundary image has length tending to 2√3 and is at least as long as the perimeter of this convex hull, the whole boundary image, and hence the band image in the Hausdorff sense used in the paper, converges to the same equilateral triangle.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2 (Topology Lemma; stated and reduced to finite estimates)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (τ1,τ2) be the tame pair associated to an embedded polygonal paper Möbius band with a chosen T-pattern in the paper's standard normalization. Here τ1 and τ2 are the two trapezoidal halves obtained after cutting along the bottom T-pattern bend, S1=L1+R1 and S2=L2+R2 are their boundary-length sums, and tameness means (S1+S2)/2<√3+10^−100. Let (0) and (6) be the bottom and top T-pattern bends. For j=1,2, let (1)j be the first bend in τj with projected pitch ±π/12, and let (4)j be the nearest bend to the top with projected pitch ±4π/12, with signs as in the paper. The 1-tip of τj is the right endpoint of the projected bend (1)j*, and the 4-tip of τj is the left endpoint of (4)j*. The notation τi→kτj means that a projected bend from τi contains the k-tip of τj, with the paper's special convention for τj→1τj using a bend in the interval from (4)j to (6). Then at least one of the following four incidences occurs: τ1→1τ1, τ2→1τ1, τ1→4τ2, or τ2→4τ1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The note does not prove this lemma directly from first principles; Section 5 reduces it to a collection of finite tensegrity calculations. The logical route is as follows. First define crossing data for projected bend images: β1|β2 if their projections meet in interiors, and β1↑β2 or β1↓β2 according as the actual spatial bend image β1 lies above or below β2 over the crossing. A triple is topologically bad if the first bend is above one of the other two but below the other. Lemma 5.1 proves that the Topology Lemma follows if certain triples are topologically good. Lemma 5.2 proves that geometric goodness, a stronger condition saying that crossings persist throughout specified one-parameter bend families, implies topological goodness. Finally, Lemmas 5.3-5.8 derive the required geometric goodness from conﬁning prism estimates and two cyclic tensegrity exclusions. Thus the stated Topology Lemma is a rigorous consequence of those finite capacity inequalities; the paper reports numerical evidence and reductions for these inequalities rather than a fully certified verification.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3 (Geometry Lemma; stated and reduced to tensegrity estimates)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (τ1,τ2) be a tame pair in the paper's standard normalization, not necessarily coming from an embedded band but coming from an immersed polygonal paper Möbius band with a T-pattern. Let S1=L1+R1 and S2=L2+R2 be the sums of the two boundary-side lengths of τ1 and τ2. With the incidence notation τi→kτj defined using projected bend images and the 1-tip or 4-tip, the paper states the following four conclusions. First, if τ1→1τ1, then S1≥√3. Second, if τ2→4τ1, then S1+S2≥2√3. Third, if τ1→1τ2 and τ2→1τ1, then S1+S2≥2√3. Fourth, if τ1→1τ2 and τ1→1τ1, then S1+S2≥2√3. The same assertions hold after interchanging the indices 1 and 2. Moreover, for every ε>0 there is δ>0 such that, if equality is approached within δ in any of these cases, then the T-pattern, or equivalently the convex hull of its two bend images as used later in the paper, is within ε in Hausdorff distance of an equilateral triangle of perimeter 2√3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Section 6 explains how the paper intends to reduce this lemma to finite cyclic tensegrity calculations. The basic mechanism is to select a small number of bends whose projected pitches are fixed or lie in the angle ranges supplied by Lemma 3.2, impose the incidence relation τi→kτj as a constraint that a selected endpoint lies on a selected projected bend, and then minimize the appropriate capacity. For the first case, the relevant cyclic tensegrity has an essential part corresponding to τ1, and the desired estimate is that the essential part has reduced capacity at least √3, with equality only in the equilateral-triangle configuration. For the second case, a cyclic tensegrity involving one bend from each trapezoid is required to have reduced capacity at least 2√3, again with equality only at the equilateral configuration. The remaining incidence combinations are to be handled by higher-complexity cyclic tensegrities encoding two interlocking incidence constraints. The proof principle is that the capacity lower bound is bounded above by S1 or S1+S2 for any actual band, so a capacity estimate gives the required metric inequality. The note presents this as a reduction and reports experimentation; it is not a completed certified proof of all the required finite inequalities.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1 (a priori bounds, quoted from [S2])
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider an immersed polygonal paper Möbius band with a T-pattern and aspect ratio less than √3, put in standard normalization. Let βb and βt be the bottom and top T-pattern bends, with image lengths B and T. Let b and t be the corresponding slopes in the planar model, so B=√(1+b²) and T=√(1+t²). Let L1,R1,L2,R2 be the four boundary-side lengths in the two trapezoids and set Sj=Lj+Rj. Let (x,y) be the displacement vector locating the top T-pattern bend relative to the normalized bottom bend as in Figure 2.1 of the paper. Let Ω be the set of possible slope pairs (b,t) for standard normalized examples with aspect ratio less than √3. Then: for j=1,2, Sj≥√3−1/24; the offset satisfies x<1/18 and |y|<1/30; and Ω is contained in an explicit quadrilateral Ωhat in the (b,t)-plane. In the later parametrization of Section 7, Ωhat is sampled by taking 0≤b≤(√27−√11)/4 and taking t between the lower line a(b)=2b/3−1/√3 and the upper piecewise-linear function u(b)=min(2b/3−1/2,4b/3−1/√3), as encoded in the note's formulas.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses distance constraints in the standard T-pattern normalization. The endpoints of the top bend are located at vertical offsets involving T/2±y from the bottom endpoint, so the Pythagorean theorem gives R1≥√(x²+(T/2−y)²) and R2≥√(x²+(T/2+y)²). Similarly, the left boundary sides satisfy L1≥√((B+x)²+(T/2+y)²) and L2≥√((B+x)²+(T/2−y)²). A subtler constraint comes from the ridge curve construction: for j=1,2, B²−Lj²+(T−Rj)²≤0. The standard planar geometry also gives Lj=(Sj+b−t)/2, Rj=(Sj−b+t)/2, B=√(1+b²), and T=√(1+t²), with the appropriate j-dependence in Sj. Substituting these relations into the inequalities yields a finite system of algebraic inequalities in S1,S2,b,t,x,y. The estimates Sj≥√3−1/24, x<1/18, |y|<1/30, and the containment Ω⊂Ωhat then follow by calculus/optimization; the details are in [S2], while this note records the constraints and conclusions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.3 (ridge curve construction)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Given a polygonal paper Möbius band cut open in a planar parallelogram and a triangulation by boundary-vertex triangles, let δ1,...,δn be the successive triangles. Define the sign μi to be −1 if δi has its ridge on the left boundary edge and +1 if its ridge is on the right boundary edge. Let γ be the core curve of the flat Möbius band, staying parallel to the boundary and at distance 1/2 from it, and let E1,...,En be the successive edge vectors of the core curve image I(γ). The ridge curve Γ is the polygonal curve whose initial vertex is (B,0,0), where B is the bottom bend length, and whose edge vectors are Γi'=2μiEi. Then Γ has total length 2λ, connects (B,0,0) to (−B,0,0), is disjoint from the open unit ball, and its side-extending lines are tangent to the unit sphere. When normalized so Γ contains (0,T,0), coning Γ to the origin gives triangles that are translates of ±I(δi) with apex at the origin, so the vectors from the origin to vertices of Γ are parallel to corresponding bend images.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The ridge curve is obtained by doubling the core-curve displacement vectors and changing their signs according to which side of the band carries the ridge. Since the core curve lies midway between the two boundary components in each flat triangle, doubling its edge vector recovers the displacement between appropriate ridge/apex-aligned boundary vertices. The sign μi records whether this displacement points toward the left or right ridge. Concatenating these signed doubled vectors turns the half-width geometry of the core into the full ridge geometry. The resulting curve connects the two endpoints associated to the bottom bend, and because each triangle of the embedded paper band is isometric to a flat triangle with width 1, the corresponding cone lines are tangent to the unit sphere and Γ stays outside the open unit ball. This construction is the source of the nontrivial inequalities used in Theorem 3.1 and Lemma 3.2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2 (angle range bounds for bend pitches)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let θ0=π/30. For any tame pair (τ1,τ2) in standard normalization, let Θ([a,b]1) denote the set of projected bend pitches attained by bends in τ1 interpolating between the special bends labeled a and b, where labels k correspond to pitch kπ/12 in τ1. Then Θ([0,1]1)⊂[−θ0,π/12], Θ([0,4]1)⊂[−θ0,4π/12+θ0], and Θ([4,6]1)⊂[4π/12,π/2+θ0]. Thus every bend pitch in τ1 lies in the slightly enlarged interval [−π/30,π/2+π/30]. The symmetric statements for τ2 follow by interchanging the two halves and reflecting signs.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The endpoint bounds without positive overshoot, such as the upper endpoint π/12 for Θ([0,1]1), follow from how the special bend (1)1 is chosen: it is the first bend after (0) with pitch π/12, so before it the pitch cannot already have crossed beyond and returned without meeting π/12 earlier. The nontrivial part is ruling out undershoot or overshoot by more than θ0. Let Γ1 be the portion of the ridge curve corresponding to τ1, connecting (B,0,0) to (0,T,0). Although its projection may leave the first quadrant, it cannot go around the back in the sense used in the note. Suppose a forbidden pitch excursion occurs. Then there are two vertical planes Π1 and Π2 through the Z-axis, separated by an angle θ≥θ0, such that Γ1 hits Π2 and later Π1 in the wrong order. Write Γ1 as Γ11∪Γ12∪Γ13, where Γ12 connects a point p in Π2 to a later point q in Π1. Reflect Γ12 across Π2 and then reflect Γ13 by the product R2R1 of reflections in Π2 and Π1. The resulting path Γ1*=Γ11∪R2(Γ12)∪R2R1(Γ13) has the same length as Γ1 but connects (B,0) to a rotated image of (0,T) whose direction differs by 2θ from the original endpoint direction. Since the ridge curve stays outside the unit ball, the projected path has length at least π/2+2θ. On the other hand, tameness plus Theorem 3.1 gives length Γ1=S1<√3+1/24 up to the negligible 10^−100 slack. Hence 2θ<√3+1/24−π/2<π/15, so θ<π/30. This contradicts any excursion by θ0 or more.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equation (10) / capacity lower bound for compound tensegrities
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Q=(Q0,Q1,Q2,Q3) be a planar trapezoid of width 1 with vertical sides, and let Q'=(Q0',Q1',Q2',Q3') be a spatial quadrilateral. Write dij=||Qi−Qj|| and dij'=||Qi'−Qj'||. The pair (Q,Q') is a plain tensegrity pair if dij≥dij' for every pair of vertices, with equality for the two bend pairs (0,1) and (2,3). The height of Q is d02+d13, the sum of the vertical side lengths, and the capacity κ(Q') is the minimum possible height of Q over all plain tensegrity pairs (Q,Q'). For an abutting compound tensegrity Ψ'=(Q0',...,Qk−1'), define κ(Ψ')=Σκ(Qi'). If Ψ1' and Ψ2' are the compound tensegrities obtained from finitely many selected bend images in the two trapezoids τ1 and τ2 of a standard normalized polygonal paper Möbius band, then S1≥κ(Ψ1') and S2≥κ(Ψ2'). For the cyclic concatenation Ψ' of the two halves, the normalization gives S1+S2≥κ(Ψ'), equivalently λ(τ1,τ2)≥κ(Ψ')/2 when λ(τ1,τ2)=(S1+S2)/2; the note writes this in Equation (10), with a slight normalization ambiguity in the displayed λ inequality. The reduced capacity κ_underbar is defined similarly but ignores the two diagonal inequalities; it satisfies κ_underbar≤κ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose consecutive bends in one trapezoid and look at the quadrilateral in the flat domain with these bends as opposite sides. Its four vertices map to a spatial quadrilateral Q'. Because I is an isometric embedding on each triangular facet and preserves intrinsic lengths, the Euclidean distance in R3 between any two image vertices is at most the intrinsic distance between the corresponding flat vertices; for the two bend pairs the segment itself is a bend and has equal length in the domain and image. Thus the actual flat quadrilateral Q and its image Q' form a tensegrity pair, so the actual height contribution of Q is at least κ(Q'). Summing over all chosen consecutive quadrilaterals in τ1 or τ2 yields S1≥κ(Ψ1') and S2≥κ(Ψ2'). For a cyclic compound tensegrity going around both trapezoids, summing the same inequalities gives the full boundary-sum estimate. Ignoring diagonal constraints only decreases the minimization problem, so lower bounds for the reduced capacity are also valid lower bounds for the true capacity if they exceed the target.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1 (Topology Lemma for topologically good pairs)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For two bend images β1 and β2, write β1|β2 if their projections to the XY-plane intersect in a point interior to both projected segments. Assuming β1|β2, write β1↑β2 if the vertical line through the projected crossing meets I(β1) above I(β2), and β1↓β2 otherwise. A triple (β1,β2,β3) is topologically bad if β1 is above one of β2,β3 and below the other at the relevant crossings; otherwise it is topologically good. The paper defines a pair (τ1,τ2) to be topologically good if specified crossings among (0), (1)j, (4)j, and (6) occur and the triples (4j,11,0), (11,4j,6), and (43−j,4j,0), with the stated exceptions corresponding to incidence relations τj→1τi or τj→4τ3−j, are topologically good. If (τ1,τ2) is topologically good, then the conclusion of the Topology Lemma holds: at least one of τ1→1τ1, τ2→1τ1, τ1→4τ2, or τ2→4τ1 occurs.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First suppose the projected bends (1)1* and (6)* do not cross. Since (1)1* crosses (4)1* and the bends in [4,6]1 vary continuously from (4)1 to (6), there must be an intermediate projected bend whose segment contains the 1-tip of τ1. This is precisely τ1→1τ1. Hence assume (1)1* crosses (6)*, and by an ambient isometry normalize the over/under choice so (1)1↓(6). There are then four possible crossing diagrams involving (0), (1)1, (4)1, and (6). One diagram contains the topologically bad triple (41,11,0), so it is forbidden. Two diagrams contain the bad triple (11,41,6), so by the definition of topological goodness they force the exceptional incidence τ1→1τ1. In the remaining diagram, introduce (4)2 and analyze four subcases according to its crossing data. One subcase gives the bad triple (41,42,0), forcing τ2→4τ1; the next is the symmetric configuration forcing τ1→4τ2; a third is impossible because it gives the bad triple (42,11,0); and the last gives the bad triple (11,42,6), forcing τ2→1τ1. These are exactly the alternatives in the Topology Lemma.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.2 (geometrical goodness implies topological goodness)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A triple (β1,β2,β3) of bends is geometrically good if β1|β for every bend β in the interpolating family between β2 and β3, where the interpolation is taken in the intended arc of bend space and is not allowed to cross the top bend T. A pair (τ1,τ2) is geometrically good if the conditions used in the definition of topological goodness hold with geometrically good replacing topologically good, including the same exceptional incidence alternatives. Then any geometrically good pair is topologically good.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implication is proved by a continuity argument. For a representative triple such as (41,11,0), imagine sweeping the bend image I(11) through the family of bends ending at I(0) while keeping I(41) fixed. If the projected crossings persist throughout the sweep, the over/under relation between the fixed bend and the moving bend cannot change without either losing the projected crossing or producing an actual spatial intersection, which embeddedness forbids. Hence the crossing signs at the two ends cannot be inconsistent, so the triple is topologically good. For a triple such as (11,41,6), the same sweep from I(41) to I(6) may allow the crossing to disappear only in the special way that the projection of one moving segment passes through the right endpoint, the 1-tip, of (1)1*. That event is exactly the incidence τ1→1τ1. Thus, except for the explicitly listed incidence alternatives, geometric persistence of crossings forces topological consistency.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 5.3-5.8 (conditional derivation of geometrical goodness from conﬁner and cyclic tensegrity estimates)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume the finite tensegrity estimates described in Sections 5.3 and 5.4: two cyclic reduced-capacity exclusions for complexity-four cyclic tensegrities, and the compound conﬁner calculations saying that the green, blue, cyan, and orange prism pairs in Figures 5.6-5.8 confine bends whose pitches lie respectively in the intervals Θ44={4π/12}, Θ01=[−π/30,π/12], Θ46=[4π/12,π/2+π/30], and Θ04=[−π/30,4π/12+π/30]. Under these estimates, the paper proves the following geometrical-goodness statements for tame pairs: Lemma 5.3, (41,11,0) is geometrically good; Corollary 5.4, (42,12,0) is geometrically good; Lemma 5.5, (42,11,0) is geometrically good; Corollary 5.6, (41,12,0) is geometrically good; Lemma 5.7, for i,j∈{1,2}, (1i,4j,6) is geometrically good unless τj→1τi; and Lemma 5.8, for i≠j, (4i,4j,0) is geometrically good unless τj→4τi. Together these statements establish geometrical goodness and hence, via Lemmas 5.1 and 5.2, the Topology Lemma, provided the listed finite estimates are verified.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The conﬁner mechanism is as follows. A prism is P×R for an open convex polygon P in the XY-plane. A prism pair confines a line segment if the segment's two endpoints lie in the two specified prisms. For a fixed pitch interval Θ and a forbidden prism halfspace H, the paper sets up a two-quadrilateral compound tensegrity calculation. If every such tensegrity with the selected endpoint in H has capacity greater than √3+1/24+10^−100, then an actual tame pair cannot place that endpoint in H: otherwise S1 would exceed √3+1/24+10^−100, while Theorem 3.1 gives the other Sj greater than √3−1/24, contradicting tameness. Applying this to all defining halfspaces of the relevant prisms confines each special bend family to the colored planar regions. Once the bends are confined, elementary planar geometry of the regions forces crossings. For instance, a green segment representing (4)1 and a blue segment representing a bend in [0,1]1 can fail to cross only if the line extending the green segment lies to the right of the whole blue segment. The cyclic capacity exclusion rules out the endpoint-on-line event that would allow the crossing status to change; since the initial bend (0)* definitely crosses, all blue segments cross the green segment, proving (41,11,0) geometrically good. The red/green/blue/magenta/orange region arguments repeat this logic for the mixed-index and upper-interval triples. In the cases (1i,4j,6) and (4i,4j,0), the same continuity argument allows exactly one exceptional event: a moving bend projection contains the relevant 1-tip or 4-tip. Those events are precisely the incidence alternatives τj→1τi and τj→4τi.

proof_techniques.name: T-pattern normalization and two-trapezoid reduction
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses Lemma 2.1 to find two perpendicular coplanar bend images in any sufficiently small-aspect embedded polygonal paper Möbius band. After choosing such a T-pattern, the band is cut open along one of the bends and normalized so that the bottom bend image is horizontal and the top bend image is vertical in the XY-plane. This turns the problem into the study of two trapezoids τ1 and τ2 with boundary-length sums S1 and S2 and a small offset vector (x,y) between the two T-pattern bends. The standard normalization also permits the definition of bend pitches and the special bends (1)j and (4)j.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem also has a flat Möbius quotient, obtained from the strip by the glide-reflection Gβ. A solver could try to find or force an analogue of a T-pattern among clean-triangulation diagonals, then reduce β to the sum of two normalized boundary-length quantities analogous to S1 and S2.

proof_techniques.name: Ridge curve as a global length and angle-control device
proof_techniques.description: The ridge curve converts the core curve of the paper Möbius band into a polygonal curve outside the unit ball whose vertices encode bend directions. Because it stays outside a convex obstacle and has controlled total length, reflection arguments across vertical planes give lower bounds on how much length is needed for bend directions to oscillate. This is how Lemma 3.2 bounds pitch ranges by allowing only π/30 of overshoot beyond the nominal intervals.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original squeeze-map setting, an analogous curve may be obtainable from the boundary/ridge combinatorics of clean triangles. Even if the map is not isometric, the squeeze inequalities may still imply an obstacle-avoidance or tangent-line condition sufficient to control pitch variation.

proof_techniques.name: Tensegrity capacity encoding of metric constraints
proof_techniques.description: The central finite-dimensional method is to choose finitely many bend images, form spatial quadrilaterals between consecutive bends, and ask for the minimum possible flat height of a planar trapezoid whose pairwise vertex distances dominate the spatial distances while bend lengths are fixed. This minimum is the capacity. Since any actual paper Möbius band supplies a feasible planar trapezoid, the actual boundary-length sum is at least the capacity. Compound and cyclic tensegrities add capacities over several adjacent quadrilaterals, reducing global length inequalities to explicit finite optimization problems.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is already formulated in terms of affine maps that shorten boundary edges and lengthen the other two edges, so selected triangles or quadrilaterals should yield direct tensegrity inequalities. One can try to define a capacity adapted to these squeeze inequalities and prove that any configuration satisfying the embedding/orbit condition has capacity at least √3.

proof_techniques.name: Reduced capacity to simplify algebra
proof_techniques.description: The paper introduces a reduced capacity by dropping the two diagonal inequalities from the quadrilateral tensegrity definition. This makes the optimization algebraically simpler while preserving a useful monotonic relation: reduced capacity is at most ordinary capacity. Therefore, any lower bound proved for reduced capacity automatically implies the same lower bound for ordinary capacity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the full squeeze-map constraints lead to difficult polynomial inequalities, a reduced-capacity relaxation may isolate the essential lower bound. A successful lower bound in the relaxed problem is stronger and easier to certify.

proof_techniques.name: Topological crossing diagrams from embeddedness
proof_techniques.description: The note projects bend images to the XY-plane and records over/under information at crossings. Embeddedness makes the over/under relation well-defined and prevents it from changing continuously unless a projected crossing disappears through a controlled endpoint event. By classifying a small number of possible crossing diagrams, the paper forces one of several incidence alternatives, such as a bend projection passing through a special tip.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The condition f(p)=f(q) if and only if p and q are in the same Gβ-orbit says that the quotient map is embedded. Projected crossings of images of clean-triangulation diagonals should therefore obey similar over/under consistency, potentially forcing the same kind of tip-on-bend incidence needed for a metric lower bound.

proof_techniques.name: Conﬁning prisms and excluder halfspaces
proof_techniques.description: To prove that certain projected bend images must cross, the paper first confines their endpoints to explicit planar regions. For each forbidden halfspace outside a desired prism, it sets up a two-quadrilateral tensegrity calculation showing that placing an endpoint there would force capacity too large for a tame pair. Intersecting the allowed halfspaces gives a prism pair for the two endpoints of a bend; elementary planar geometry of the confined regions then forces crossing patterns.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests a modular proof plan: first use local edge inequalities to confine possible positions of images of selected clean-triangulation vertices, then use planar convex geometry plus embeddedness to force incidences. Such a separation could make the original problem more tractable.

proof_techniques.name: Finite parametrization of constrained bend configurations
proof_techniques.description: Section 7 describes how to parametrize tensegrities by finitely many variables in a cube [0,1]^N. Variables encode the slopes of the bottom and top T-pattern bends, the offset (x,y), the centers, pitches, and vertical tilts of middle bends, and the incidence constraints. The a priori bounds from Theorem 3.1 and Lemma 3.2 keep the domains compact and low-dimensional enough for numerical or certified algebraic checking.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original problem, after selecting a finite periodic set of vertices or diagonals in a Gβ-invariant triangulation, one may similarly parametrize all possible normalized images and reduce the √3 lower bound to positivity of explicit functions on a compact cube.

proof_techniques.name: Near-equality rigidity from equality cases of capacity
proof_techniques.description: The Geometry Lemma is designed not only to prove lower bounds S1≥√3 or S1+S2≥2√3, but also to identify the equality case: the convex hull of the T-pattern must be an equilateral triangle of perimeter 2√3. This converts quantitative lower bounds into Hausdorff convergence of near-minimizers.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Even if the original problem only asks for the infimum of realized β, proving a rigidity statement for near-realizers may simplify the lower bound by showing that any minimizing sequence must converge to the same equilateral-triangle model that supplies the upper bound.

other_useful_info: The paper emphasizes that embeddedness is the key difficulty: immersed paper Möbius bands can have aspect ratios approaching π/2, so metric inequalities alone are insufficient. It cites Halpern-Weaver for the classical bound λ0∈[π/2,√3], Fuchs-Tabachnikov for exposition and the approximating examples near √3, and Schwartz [S2] for a prior rigorous lower bound slightly stronger than √3−1/26 and for the a priori estimates quoted here. The note is explicitly described by the author as research notes rather than a finished paper; many tensegrity estimates are reduced to positivity of explicit piecewise algebraic expressions, with numerical experimentation reported and code posted at the author's website. The presumed limiting optimizer throughout is an equilateral triangle of semiperimeter √3, i.e. perimeter 2√3.
why_relevant: Although superseded by the later Annals proof, this research note develops a finite tensegrity/edge-inequality viewpoint. That is especially close in spirit to the problem’s squeeze maps, where boundary edges are shortened and transverse edges lengthened.

title: Isometric Immersions and Embeddings of a Flat Möbius Strip in Euclidean Spaces
why_relevant: Sabitov gives explicit formulae and geometric analysis for isometric immersions and embeddings of flat Möbius bands. This is useful background for comparing the quotient of the strip by the glide reflection with concrete embedded models.

title: Construction of Unknotted and Knotted Symmetric Developable Bands
why_relevant: This paper provides explicit constructions of symmetric developable bands, including Möbius bands, using cylinder-scaffold ideas descending from Sadowsky. Such constructions can help prove the realizability side for aspect ratios above the critical value.
